c2ju1CvnakE.title: 4 Powerful Supplements to Lower Blood Sugar (Backed by Science!)
c2ju1CvnakE.description: Are you fed up with the side effects and sky-high costs of blood sugar medications? What if you could manage your blood sugar ...
c2ju1CvnakE.transcript: Are you tired of dealing with the side effects and high costs of blood sugar medications? What if I told you there are natural, affordable alternatives that can help you manage your blood sugar—without the nasty side effects or monthly prescriptions? In today’s video, I’m going to share 5 supplements that can lower blood sugar naturally—helping you feel better, save money, and reduce reliance on medications. I’m Dr. Morgan Nolte, founder of Zivli where we help you lose weight, keep it off, and get healthy with a low insulin and inflammation lifestyle. If you’re new here, make sure to hit that subscribe button and notification bell so you never miss out on tips that help you live a healthier, happier life. Many of us don’t realize how powerful supplements can be in managing blood sugar—alongside diet, exercise, and lifestyle changes like reducing your stress and improving your sleep. Remember, they are to SUPPLEMENT lifestyle changes. You cannot circumvent the required lifestyle changes to lose weight, keep it off, and improve your health. You cannot outmedicate or outsupplement poor sleep, chronic stress, a bad diet, or no exercise. Before we dive in, be sure to run any supplement you want to take by your physician to be sure it won’t interact with something you’re already taking - especially if you’re currently taking blood sugar lowering medications. You’ll want to be on the lookout for signs of low blood sugar like cool, clammy skin, shakiness, blurry vision, confusion, and fatigue. Keep a log of your blood sugars when you start a new supplement, and be in communication with your physician about them so that you can be on the same page for when it’s time to reduce or stop a blood sugar medication to avoid dangerous blood sugar lows. Let’s start with cinnamon. And I’m not just talking about sprinkling it on your oatmeal! Cinnamon has been shown to improve insulin sensitivity, meaning it helps your body use insulin more effectively and stabilize blood sugar levels. One study even found that cinnamon can lower blood sugar by up to 29% when taken regularly! Isn’t that amazing? To see noticeable effects, aim for 1 to 6 grams of cinnamon daily—about 1/2 to 1 teaspoon. Opt for Ceylon cinnamon over cassia cinnamon. Cassia is more commonly found in the grocery store, but has larger amounts of coumarin which can be toxic in high doses. Ceylon cinnamon has less coumarin and more antioxidants, so is a better choice for the higher dose required to lower blood sugar. Next up is berberine, a powerful supplement that’s been getting a lot of attention lately. Derived from various plants, berberine has been shown to help regulate glucose metabolism and even improve insulin sensitivity. One meta-analysis suggested that “There was no statistical significance in the hypoglycemic effect between berberine and metformin for type 2 diabetes.” Studies suggest it’s just as effective as some diabetic medications, but without the uncomfortable side effects. If you’re looking for a natural way to lower blood sugar, berberine is definitely one to try. My recommended supplement is Super Berberine by SugarMD. It contains a form of berberine called dihydroberberine, which is more bioavailable than other forms, and contains highly concentrated ceylon cinnamon. You can find a link to SuperBerberine in the description. The next supplement is magnesium—a crucial mineral that plays a huge role in blood sugar regulation. Magnesium plays a role in practically all cellular processes. Overall, around 80% of all metabolic reactions are dependent on Magnesium. Signs of a magnesium deficiency include muscle cramps, headache, irregular heart rhythms, high blood pressure, sleep disturbances, depression, complications in pregnancy, insulin resistance, and higher blood sugar levels. Good sources of magnesium include almonds, peanuts, cashews, pumpkin seeds, peanut butter, black and kidney beans, soybeans, soy milk, cooked spinach, Swiss chard, white potato with skin, brown rice, oatmeal, salmon, beef, poultry, banana, raisins, dark chocolate (at least 70%), milk, and yogurt. But even with a healthy diet, it can be easy to have a magnesium deficiency. This is one mineral I supplement regularly either via electrolyte packets or a capsule. There are different forms of magnesium and the type does matter. For example, Magnesium citrate is best to help with constipation. There is also magnesium citrate, glycinate, and oxide among others. And many of these forms help with blood sugar regulation. I recommend magnesium glycinate or bisglycinate as a good overall magnesium form to take. Lastly, we have chromium, a trace mineral that helps your body process carbohydrates and fats more efficiently, which in turn helps lower blood sugar. Some studies show that chromium improves insulin sensitivity, making it an excellent choice for those looking to enhance their blood sugar management. And one study showed that taking chromium and magnesium together decreased insulin resistance (which remember is the cause of high blood sugars) more effectively than taking either magnesium or chromium alone. To recap, the 4 supplements that can help with blood sugar management are cinnamon, berberine, magnesium, and chromium. These are all research-backed supplements that can support your blood sugar control and improve insulin sensitivity. Remember to always consult with your healthcare provider before starting any new supplement, especially if you have existing health conditions or are on medication for diabetes. Supplementing alongside your medications may lead to a sudden and significant reduction in blood sugar levels, or have adverse effects for other medications you’re taking. So, what do you think? Have you tried any of these, or are there other supplements you’ve heard of that help with blood sugar management? Type ‘YES’ in the comments if you’ve used any of these or if you plan to try them out! AzB25i29DkA.title: Do Testosterone "Boosters" Actually Work?
AzB25i29DkA.description: See the structures in real-time at our upcoming Live Broadcast Workshop! Register Here: ...
AzB25i29DkA.transcript: Testosterone continues to be quite the hot topic. And there seems to be new testosterone clinics popping up everywhere, but then there's the supplement industry with products claiming to naturally boost your testosterone, helping you to run faster, jump higher, and have a libido that is not for the faint of heart. But in order for something to naturally boost testosterone, it would somehow have to stimulate these wonderful structures, the testes, to produce more of it. But what if I told you the testes don't actually decide how much testosterone you make. Your brain does. And not only that, your body is actively designed to prevent your testosterone from getting too high. So that raises a very important question. If testosterone production is controlled by a communication system between your brain and testes, what would it actually take for a supplement to increase it? And how does this compare to taking actual testosterone? Well, today we're going to answer these questions as well as talk about some of the most common supplements that claim to boost testosterone and of course see if any of them actually work. It's going to be a testicular one. So, let's jump into this anatomical and physiological awesomeness. When people think about testosterone, they usually think about the testes. And that's a good place to start because if we look at this testice right here, this is where the majority of testosterone in the male body is produced. Inside the testes are structures called seminifpherous tubules where sperm are produced. And to the naked eye, these seminifpherous tubules look like string-like structures. But between those tubules are cells called lidic cells, which produce the testosterone. But here's an important concept that will also help us understand why raising testosterone isn't so simple. The testes don't decide on their own how much testosterone to produce. That process is actually controlled by the brain. In the central core of the brain, there's a region called the hypothalammus, which helps regulate hormone production throughout the body. The hypothalammus sends signals to a small gland below it called the pituitary gland. And then the pituitary releases hormones that travel through the bloodstream to the testes. One of those hormones is luteinizing hormone or LH which stimulates the lidic cells to produce testosterone. So testosterone production is really part of a communication system between the brain and the testes. The hypothalammus sends signals to the pituitary, the pituitary sends signals to the testes and then the testes produce testosterone. Now once testosterone levels start to increase, you don't want those levels to just keep rising indefinitely. So the body regulates this through what is known as negative feedback. And one of the best ways to understand negative feedback is to compare it to the thermostat and furnace in your house. If you set your thermostat to 70°, but your house is only 60°, your thermostat is going to sense that colder temperature and tell your furnace to kick on or secrete the heat. But again, you don't want your furnace to keep secretreting heat indefinitely because it would get way too hot. So, as you know, once the temperature gets to 70°, the thermostat senses this and shuts off the furnace, but it will kick back on again if the levels of heat get too low again. And this is essentially how negative feedback works in the human body. Think of the hypothalammus and the pituitary as the thermostat that tells the furnace, the testes, to secrete testosterone when levels are too low. And then once testosterone levels get high enough, they will sense this and tell the testes to shut off. So, this isn't just one organ working together. It's a tightly regulated endocrine system involving the hypothalamus, the pituitary gland, and the testes. And that raises some important questions. If testosterone production depends on signaling between the brain and the testes, what would it actually take for a supplement to increase testosterone levels? And if you take actual testosterone in the form of an injection or cream from an outside source, how do you think the pituitary and hypothalammus are going to respond to those higher levels? Do you think they will keep telling your testes to produce testosterone? Well, because of this feedback system, testosterone isn't like caffeine where you can just consume a supplement and immediately raise levels, unless we're talking about injecting actual testosterone, which we'll get into after we talk about the supplements. And so because the body actively regulates testosterone through communication between the hypothalamus, pituitary, and the testes, supplements would need to influence some part of this hormonal system. Either the signaling coming from the brain, the testes themselves, or the metabolic pathways that regulate testosterone levels. And that's a pretty big biological claim, as these products are usually marketed as alternatives to prescription hormone therapy. often claiming they can increase testosterone, improve muscle growth, increase libido, and boost energy levels. So, let's look at some of the most common supplements that you hear about and see if they have any chance of increasing testosterone. First up, we have zinc. One that you might not typically think about with testosterone, but zinc is a mineral involved in hundreds of enzyatic reactions throughout the body. Zinc plays a role in normal testosterone production because it is involved in several enzyatic steps that are used to synthesize testosterone. Studies show that men who are zinc deficient can have lower testosterone levels, and correcting that deficiency may help bring testosterone levels back toward normal. But if someone is deficient in zinc, there are plenty of other reasons to correct that deficiency beyond just testosterone. However, if someone already has adequate zinc levels or their low testosterone is not related to zinc deficiency, supplementing with additional zinc is unlikely to increase testosterone levels. Another nutrient that frequently appears in testosterone boosters is vitamin D. Vitamin D actually behaves more like a hormone than a traditional vitamin. Its active form binds to vitamin D receptors that are found throughout the entire body, including in the testes, particularly in cells involved in testosterone production. Because of this, vitamin D may influence some of the genes and enzymes involved in steroid hormone synthesis. Some studies have found that men with low vitamin D levels tend to have lower testosterone levels. And correcting a vitamin D deficiency may help restore testosterone levels toward normal in some individuals. But again, like zinc, there are other reasons to correct a vitamin D deficiency beyond just testosterone. And if somebody already has adequate vitamin D levels or their low testosterone isn't related to vitamin D deficiency, supplementing with additional vitamin D is unlikely to significantly increase testosterone levels. But next, let's talk about two supplements that get a lot of attention these days with boosting testosterone. Tonkatut Ali and Ashwagandha. But before we get to those two supplements, I need to tell you about a sponsor that we have never used before in one of our YouTube videos. And that sponsor is me. because I'm going to be doing a cadaavver workshop here in the lab that you can attend from anywhere because it's going to be streamed live. We are going to look at multiple bodies so that we can cover different muscles, organs, and different tissues. You are essentially going to be part of what we do with our students that come into the actual lab and learn anatomy from the cadaavvers, which means you'll get even more detail about how the human body is really put together. So, if you're interested and want to be part of this anatomical awesomeness, the dates and details will be down in the description. Now, there is a limit to the number of people that we can fit into the live broadcast. So, if you want to do this, be sure to sign up quickly because Jeffrey the skeleton is super excited to interact with as many of you as possible. So, we hope to see you there. And now, let's get back to those testosterone supplements. Tonka Ali is becoming increasingly more popular. It's thought to potentially influence testosterone through a few different pathways. Some research suggests it may reduce sex hormone binding globbulin, which is a protein that binds testosterone in the bloodstream. So, if there's less sex hormone binding globbulin, there could potentially be more free testosterone available. There are also hypotheses that ton ali may influence hormone signaling pathways involving the hypothalamus, pituitary, and testes, which as we know regulate testosterone production. And a few small studies have reported increases in testosterone or improvements in certain symptoms particularly in men experiencing stress or age related hormonal changes. But these studies are generally small and the results have not been consistently replicated across larger trials. So while Tonkit Ali is often marketed as a testosterone booster, the evidence so far is still limited and not definitive. Another supplement that gets a lot of attention these days is ashwagandha. An herb traditionally described as an adaptogen, meaning it may help the body regulate its response to stress. So rather than directly stimulating testosterone production, ashwagandha appears to work primarily through its effects on stress physiology. Several studies have shown that it can reduce levels of cortisol, which is the body's primary stress hormone. And cortisol matters here because chronically elevated cortisol can interfere with the hormonal signaling pathways that regulate testosterone production. So by lowering cortisol and improving the body's stress response, ashwagandha may help support overall hormonal balance which could allow testosterone levels to normalize in some individuals. Some studies in men under high stress or undergoing resistance training have reported small increases in testosterone, but the effects are generally modest and not consistently seen across all studies. So, while ashwagandha is often marketed as a testosterone booster, its potential benefits may be more related to stress reduction and hormonal balance rather than directly stimulating the testes to produce more testosterone. And finally, there's depppartic acid, an amino acid that gained a lot of attention several years ago as a possible testosterone booster. It was proposed to increase testosterone by influencing hormone signaling in the brain. Specifically, it may stimulate the release of luteinizing hormone from the pituitary gland, which, as we discussed earlier, signals the testes to produce testosterone. Some earlier studies reported increases in testosterone levels, which is part of the reason the supplement became popular. However, later research in healthy men has generally failed to consistently reproduce those results. So, while the proposed mechanism is interesting because it targets the hormonal signaling pathway that regulates testosterone production, the overall evidence so far suggests limited and inconsistent effects. So, what should we do with all this information? Are these testosterone supplements a scam? Well, scam is probably too strong of a word because there is data that shows some possible benefits with these supplements, especially in situations of certain nutrient deficiencies and high stress. And if we did make a list of which ones appear to be the most effective, we would have ashwagandha at the top, which again may have modest effects, especially in high stress individuals. Then Tonat Ali, which is promising, but still a little inconsistent. And then we have zinc and vitamin D, which really only help if you're deficient. But I'll actually touch a little bit more on vitamin D and vitamin D deficiency in just a second. And finally, daspartic acid, which sounds great in theory. However, it doesn't consistently hold up. But does that mean you should take them as this general testosterone insurance policy? And what I mean by that is there are some schools of thought where people will want to take supplements kind of as an insurance policy or as a just in case I'm deficient, I'll take these to try to optimize my natural testosterone production. Now, this is not the worst thing that you can do and there are people who have reported feeling better taking some of these supplements. And out of all the supplements we have talked about, vitamin D probably has the strongest argument for the insurance policy approach. And that's because it's estimated that 20 to 30% of people in the US are truly deficient with 50 to 80% of people getting insufficient amounts. And again, there are other important physiological processes that vitamin D is involved in other than just testosterone production. Zinc deficiency, however, is much less common. And the other three are not in this same nutrient category as zinc and vitamin D. Meaning, it's not like you can be deficient in tonget ali or ashwagandha. and your body can actually synthesize diaspartic acid. But again, could someone take this insurance policy approach and get some benefits? Yeah, it's possible. And these supplements are generally considered safe. But it's still a bit of a shot in the dark without definitive testing, and one would have to accept the potential for a placebo effect or even no effect at all. But us humans, we're kind of funny because when we hear something could potentially help, and especially when us guys hear it could potentially help with our testosterone, we're like, >> "So, you're telling me there's a chance?" >> Yeah. >> But the best thing to do would be to test your testosterone levels and even potentially vitamin D levels. Zinc isn't typically tested for as again, it's quite uncommon to be low, but once you get your test results, you could assess from there. If your vitamin D level is low, obviously you could supplement with vitamin D for multiple reasons. And if your testosterone level is low and you don't want to take exogenous testosterone, then you could maybe try one of these supplements and then retest to see if it actually raised your levels. Again, these would likely be relatively small improvements in testosterone levels. And part of the reason for that goes back to the physiology we talked about earlier. Testosterone production is tightly regulated by communication between the hypothalammus, pituitary, and the testes. And this system uses negative feedback to keep levels within a normal range. So even if a supplement influences part of that system, the body will often adjust its signaling to prevent large increases and ensure testosterone stays within a normal range, which most people are kind of like, that's fine. I just want my levels to be normal anyway. However, this is where we get into the discussion of how these supplements compare to testosterone replacement therapy. Testosterone replacement therapy, often called TRT, is very different from the over-the-counter supplements because you are not trying to support or bolster any of the physiological steps in the pathway. You are bypassing all those steps and going straight to the end product by giving someone actual exogenous testosterone. Exogenous just means it's coming from a source outside of the body. And this involves prescription testosterone, typically delivered through injections, gels, patches, or pellets. This treatment is intended for men with confirmed hypogonatism, which means the body is not producing normal levels of testosterone due to a problem with the testes or some step in the hormonal signaling pathway. But TRT isn't something that's prescribed based on symptoms alone. Doctors usually confirm low testosterone levels through blood tests, often repeated more than once, and then evaluate whether those levels and symptoms fit the diagnosis. And like any medical therapy, TRT has some pros and some cons. One of the pros is that it predictably works because again, you are literally giving people testosterone and so you can substantially increase testosterone levels and people with hypogonatism or low testosterone will often feel much better. But again, there are cons or trade-offs. It can suppress sperm production and affect fertility. And we actually learned why that is. Remember the hypothalammus and the pituitary gland. When you give someone exogenous testosterone, the hypothalammus and the pituitary gland don't know the difference. So what happens is they kind of think, oh, we've got plenty of testosterone, so no need to have the testes produce any more of it. So essentially, they reduce their signaling, specifically lowering the release of hormones that normally tell the testes to produce testosterone. And now you have potentially diminished your own natural testosterone production because you're continually getting it from an outside source. And this is what leads to infertility and even testicular atrophy, meaning the testes can get smaller. And there are other potential side effects such as ariththroytosis, which is too many red blood cells being produced. It can also lead to acne, mood swings, enlarged prostate, and hair loss in those that are genetically predisposed. Now, I do need to say there are nuances to this, meaning there are additional medications that someone can take to often help counteract some of these side effects such as testicular atrophy and to try to keep the whole hormonal system more in balance. But the details of those nuances, we'll do in a different video that's more dedicated to TRT. But one very common question you hear from people who are considering taking testosterone is, "Will I have to be on it indefinitely?" And the answer is possibly. With prolonged use, there is a chance that natural testosterone production may not fully return to baseline, especially after long-term therapy. If you're in middle age or later in life, maybe that's not as big of a deal to you. But if you're younger and you're wanting to have kids, this becomes a more important consideration. And this is one of the reasons why sometimes these supplements can seem appealing for someone to try first because unlike exogenous testosterone, they generally do not suppress natural testosterone production in a meaningful way, even if their effects are modest. But ultimately, this is going to be a very personal decision and sometimes a decision between you and a health care provider, especially if you decide to explore the TRT route. So whether you're looking at supplements or testosterone therapy, it all comes back to understanding how this system actually works. Because once you understand the anatomy and physiology, you can make better decisions about how to influence it. And I hope I provided you with enough information that can help you with this process. So thank you so much for watching and supporting our channel. And as always, we'll see you in the next video.

ingredients and see if it actually works here's the list of ingredients and their doses per capsule we'll analyze whether these doses are effective and if there's any evidence it can enhance exercise performance or improve libido B vitamins zinc and Boron are essential nutrients for supporting healthy muscle growth and exercise for those who engage in intense workout routines but does supplementing with these nutrients actually improve muscle growth and performance for B vitamins research shows that taking folic acid and vitamin B12 daily for 2 years does not increase hand strength or improve physical performance like walking compared to a placebo in the case of zinc a study found that giving zinc and selenium to people on a weight loss program did not change their body composition or exercise performance as for Boron a small study involving male bodybuilders showed that taking Boron did not increase lean muscle mass or testosterone levels compared to a placebo so if these nutrients are important for exercise why don't supplements seem to help the key is moderation while it's crucial to have a diet rich in these nutrients during training taking more than you need through supplements doesn't lead to extra muscle growth or performance improvements there's also no evidence that supplementing with these nutrients affects testosterone levels or libido in humans El citrine is a non-essential amino acid found in watermelon squash and pumpkins it was first isolated from watermelon for resistance exercises like weightlifting research shows that taking L citruline before Anor robic exercise slightly reduces perceived exhaustion and muscle soreness compared to a placebo other studies indicate that it may slightly increase the amount of repetitions until exhaustion for resistance exercises however a clinical study found that L citrine does not increase actual lean muscle mass or muscle strength compared to a placebo for aerobic exercises the research is mixed for running a small study discovered that taking El citrine before a treadmill test did not improve performance but might reduce time to exhaustion compared to a placebo however for cycling a small study found that taking L citrine daily before a cycling test did slightly improve EX size intensity and performance compared to a placebo however it did not enhance recovery times so research suggests that taking L citruline before Anor robic resistance exercise might reduce exhaustion and improve some performance measures like repetitions but likely won't actually increase muscle mass or strength for aerobic exercise the evidence is less clear with conflicting results and might depend on the type of aerobic exercise you're doing also these studies use doses of el citrine malate ranging from 3 to 9 G before exercise with 8 G being the most common dosage nenic Total Tea provides just over 1 G which is not enough to be effective additionally there is no evidence that El citrine improves testosterone levels or libido in humans fenugreek is an aromatic herb native to the Mediterranean region a small clinical study found that taking a specific fenugreek extract for 8 weeks while training slightly decreases body fat and may improve leg and bench press performance compared to a placebo a men analyses of small trials showed that fenugreek extracts May improve various aspects of athletic performance and reduce body fat additionally these studies found an increase in testosterone levels for libido Research indicates that taking fenugreek seed extract for 12 weeks in older men aged 43 to 70 slightly increases libido compared tobo other research on middle-age to older men age 35 to 60 with low testosterone levels shows that taking fenugreek for 12 weeks improves libido and mood compared to Baseline so research suggests that fenugreek extracts can potentially enhance athletic performance and may increase testosterone levels especially in older men or those with low testosterone to start with this could lead to some improvements in libido for these populations the proprietary blend of Pete and apple fruit extract known as elevatp has been weekly studied in two small trials funded by the manufacturer in one trial men undergoing resistance training while taking this blend showed some improvements in various measures of athletic performance but how significant were these improvements if we look at the actual study data and calculate approximately how much these values improved compared to the placebo the average Improvement was about 3.6% this means that taking elevatp daily for 12 weeks only improved athletic performance by a measly 3.6% compared to a placebo which isn't very impressive especially considering the study was funded by the manufacturer in the second trial they focus on body composition measuring factors like body weight body fat and muscle thickness the study claimed that the blend might increase muscle size without affecting fat Mass however when we examine the actual data we see no significant change in weight or body fat percentage there was a small 7.6% increase in muscle thickness but it was accompanied by an 8.7% increase in fat thickness so the blend did not significantly increase muscle mass without affecting fat mass and the change in both muscle and fat were minimal anyways so to summarize while Lev ATP has conducted studies on their blend which is more more than many other supplements the results show that its effects on athletic performance and muscle size are tiny you're unlikely to notice any real difference and if you do gain some muscle you'll probably gain some fat too Yuri coma Longoria also known as Tong cat Aly or long Jack is a tree native to Southeast Asia while it has been studied for its potential effects on athletic performance the results are mostly disappointing in a small study involving cyclists taking ton cat alley daily didn't significantly improv their endurance compared to a placebo similarly research on male athletes found no noticeable Improvement in running performance with ton cat alley another study focused on men showed that while a particular brand of ton cat alley extract did not enhance physical fitness it did appear to boost libido there's a theory that tonat alley May slightly boost testosterone levels however this in itself doesn't appear to increase libido in healthy younger men only men with existing libido issues seem to notice a difference so tonat alley probably does not enhance physical fitness or performance but it may slightly increase testosterone levels and boost libido particularly in those with libido issues to begin with Additionally the doses used in these studies are higher than those found in nugenics total te overall nugenics total te might have some small benefits on some aspects of exercise performance and testosterone levels and Men primarily due to the fenu Greek extract and ton cat alley it contains however it's unlikely to increase actual muscle mass and the small increases in testosterone and libido are likely to affect mostly older men with low testosterone issues to begin with Total Tea costs around $60 per month if you wanted to replicate its effects you would combine a fenu Greek extract with ton cat alley a generic fenu greet extract costs just $3 to5 a month and ton cat alley is $6 to $8 a month together that's only $9 to $13 a month rating nugenics total T by Effectiveness for improving exercise performance and boosting testosterone I'm giving it a c it may have some weak effects on exercise performance though you likely won't notice any difference in muscle mass it may also boost testosterone but mostly in men with low testosterone levels to begin with rating it by cost I'm giving it a d you can easily replicate its effects with generic ingredients at a much lower cost rant for safety I'm giving it a c fenre can lower blood sugar and blood pressure levels potentially interacting with anti-diabetic or anti-hypertensive medications if you're taking them overall rating I'm giving a c it might slightly improve exercise performance and boost testosterone levels if they're low but taking fenu Greek extract by itself will likely do the same thing at a far cheaper cost hi I'm Dr Brian Young do you think it's worth it let me know in the comments down below and if you found this video helpful please subscribe to the channel and leave me a like hit the notification Bell to stay up the date and share this video with some no used info [Music]

test and essentially how we test for low testosterone you find that somewhere between 230 to 250 nanog per deciliter you're starting to see erectile dysfunction and decrease frequency of sexual thoughts when you get a little higher than that around 320 you'll start seeing a decreased frequency of morning erections in terms of physical symptoms at about 370 you will start noticing that you're having decreased Vigor or energy and so very you know it's very correlated with the amount of testosterone that you have when you develop these symptoms so when do we treat as I mentioned you must have symptoms that are bothersome to you as well as low testosterone on a blood test so typically you'll do a total testosterone level but it's important to realize the testosterone by itself is not the indicator of how much testosterone is actually available to your body because a lot of it is bound the large majority of it is bound with something called sex hormone binding globulin as well as cortisol binding globulin and so it only remains a very small percentage that's available for free testosterone so that can also be evaluated as well as a sex hormone binding globulin to assess your total testosterone or actual free testosterone level you can also assess other hormone levels that could be affecting testosterone that can include estrodial prolactin maybe your LH and FSH to sort of assess if there are other factors that are contributing to low testosterone as well as your thyroid hormone because that can also play a role now if you are wondering about testosterone replacement therapy which we're going to talk about you cannot get testosterone replacement therapy if you have active prostate cancer if you have untreated sleep apnea or uncontrolled sleep apnea if you have a hematocrit or blood count of over 50 if you're planning to be uh have pregnancy or have a child because it can cause infertility and then if you have an elevated PSA or blood test that's testing for screening for prostate cancer that may be concerning you need to get that evaluated before you start any sort of testosterone replacement now how do you boost your testosterone naturally the first thing is exercise so we know that exercise particularly resistance training of large muscle groups can cause an increase in testosterone now it's not a prolonged increase in testosterone so typically you have to incorporate this resistance training in your regular week to week exercise regimen now you want to be careful not to do really high endurance like ultramarathons marathons if you're having symptoms because those sorts of exercises actually increase your body's cortisol which increases stress and can actually reduce your testosterone they've also done some studies that have shown that high-intensity interval training can be beneficial for testosterone now nutrition the best data we have on nutrition is with the Mediterranean diet and so that's generally a diet that's high in poultry and fish vegetables healthy fats including sort of lowfat dairy olive oils avocados and nuts as well as trying to get like sort of healthy and unprocessed Foods so you want to avoid processed foods and include healthy fats in your diet sometimes people go for a very lowfat diet and that actually is not beneficial for testosterone because testosterone is made through the cholesterol Pathways and you need some healthy fat in order to get good testosterone production now weight loss so there's a whole host of studies that have shown that weight loss improves testosterone so some Studies have shown that you need quite a large amount of weight loss like 46 lb weight loss or if you incorporate physical activity it can be 26 lbs but the best study is about a 10% reduction in body weight can show a pretty significant Improvement in testosterone levels they've also shown studies that people have had quite tremendous increases in testosterone after bariatric surgery for those who are obese and candidates for bariatric surgery and even an improvement in your body mass index of five points for those who are overweight or obese can be quite uh remarkable in terms of significantly improving testosterone now sleep is another really important area and this is because during the night is when our body our bodies work in a circadian rhythm and during the night time is when we sort of allow the body to rest and rejuvenate and then allow the surge in the morning of early testosterone and so each hour of sleep lost decreases your testosterone by about six points and they've shown that when they compare men who sleep about 5 hours a night versus those who sleep about eight or more hours a night they will see a 10 to 15% decrease in testosterone now if you have sleep apnea that's also correlated with lowest testosterone and using a c app has also shown to significantly improve testosterone levels by about 90 to 100 points reducing stress now stress is unavoidable but we've seen that people who have high levels of work stress meaning they're commuting more than an hour to work they may be working for more than one job they may have more deadlines or sales quotas or they have stressors about layoffs tend to have lower levels of testosterone also they did one study where they looked at resident doctors who were stressed and also sleeping less meaning sleeping about every four nights they' be on call that they found that their testosterones were significantly lower despite being typically young healthy men compared to other Hospital workers when it came to testosterone levels so if it's possible reducing stress can be very impactful in terms of testosterone avoiding endocrine disrupting chemicals so things like uh bpas which are found in Plastic Products or phalates which are found in plastic tubing uh have been shown to significantly negatively impact the hormone production of testosterone and sperm production so ultimately trying to avoid those as much as possible what I tell people is drinking from a glass water bottle or a metal bottle water bottle of plastic heat your food up in um glass plates instead of using the plastic containers don't use plastic Tupperware those are sort of the things that we can control and can help improve our exposures but unfortunately everyone is exposed and has some degree of these endocrine disrupting chemicals in their blood levels or urine that we can measure there are a whole host of different types of testosterones that are available for testosterone replacement therapy if these other Alternatives do not work they can include injections which can be done every other day to twice a week to weekly to every 10 days depending on your preference the nice thing about this is there is flexibility on how often you get the dosing It's relatively afford able however it is an injection so normally the male body makes about 5 Mig of testosterone every day but when you take it injected you're not getting that daily dose you're getting sort of a a larger dose at one time and then that sort of goes up and comes down there's transdermal gels which are you put on your arm typically daily there's patches you put on your body daily now these have some risk of the gels of transferring the testosterone to other people the patches sometimes people get skin irritation from the patches there's also Buckle tablets which means you like sort of rub them on your gums but you can have gum related side effects you can get pellets implanted and these essentially are implanted every 4 months or so and they release testas for an extend a period of time however they do have sort of Peaks and valleys of testosterone so very early when you get the pellets you may feel a very high rate of testosterone which will then sort of fizzle out over that course of 4 months there's also long acting injectables which are injected usually every 4 weeks and then every 10 weeks and again same thing with the levels going sort of up and down there's a nasal spray that you can do about three times a day and that can have some nasal side effects and there's now oral options available as well which are twice daily now both the nasal and oral have a rapid onset so you typically see symptom relief very quickly when you take the medication but it also wears off pretty Um things that I have seen and things that can happen are that any exogenous testosterone that is produced or introduced into a young person's system. It interferes with the normal balance of hormones. It usually most often adversely affects the millu. yoP6NCGb3oM.title: How to Increase Testosterone Naturally | Science Explained
yoP6NCGb3oM.description: For 10% off your first purchase, go to: ‣ http://squarespace.com/nippard ------------------------------- ▹ CHECK OUT MY TRAINING ...
yoP6NCGb3oM.transcript: [Music] A few years ago, a bodybuilder friend of mine from the gym told me it's really important that I do squats, deadlifts, and other heavy compound exercises because they boost testosterone more than lightweight movements or machine exercises. And it turns out in a way he was right. It's long been established that resistance exercise increases serum concentrations of hormones like testosterone, growth hormone, and IGF-1. And foundational research from the 1990s and early 2000s led by Dr. Bill Kramer showed that the higher the training intensity, so training with heavier loads and the more muscle mass involved in the exercise, so training with compound movements for example, the more testosterone increase you see. And the reasoning from there was fairly straightforward since we know that testosterone is anabolic, aiming to increase it acutely should be weighted pretty heavily when it comes to designing an effective hypertrophy focused training program. And naturally, I went on believing for several years that the reason why this style of training was so effective for me was that it was jacking up my testosterone. It wasn't until later that I realized that the level of testosterone increase seen with these training modifications just isn't enough to increase muscle growth on its own. And that this heavy compound strategy is most likely effective for a different reason. It induces progressive tension overload and activates a large volume of musculature with the modest testosterone increase being a mere side effect, so to speak. And this is the more consensus view in the current scientific literature as more recent data from Western colleagues out of McMaster University concluded that post exercise increases in these hormones cannot be used as proxy markers for hypertrophic potential in human sceal muscle. And if you just look at some of their data, it's clear that despite the fact that testosterone did increase with a training protocol that emphasized high intensity and large muscle masses, there was no difference in muscle protein synthesis between the groups. And just keep in mind that the increases in testosterone were acute, meaning they didn't get elevated from the training and stay elevated. They simply spiked up and then came back down after an hour, which seems almost definitely not enough of a chronic hormonal shift to do much for muscle growth. So reading this made me curious how much of an increase you'd need to see gains from it. So let's talk some numbers. According to a 2017 paper, a harmonized normal range for healthy non-obese American and European men aged 19 to 39 years old is 264 to 916 nanogs per deciliter. And it's not clear if increasing testosterone naturally within this range will do much in the way of increasing muscle mass. However, we do know that increasing it through exogenous testosterone supplementation does increase muscle mass. In fact, it might increase muscle mass better than training alone does. A landmark 1996 paper published in the New England Journal of Medicine split 40 men into one of four groups. Group one trained naturally. Group two took 600 mg of testosterone every week and weight trained. Group three took 600 millig of testosterone every week but did not weight train. And group four was the placebo group. They didn't train or inject testosterone. The training program was a little weird. Just four sets of six reps on the squat and bench press three times a week for 10 weeks. But some of the results were pretty shocking. The group that injected testosterone and trained saw unsurprisingly the most gains and the placebo group saw unsurprisingly the worst gains. However, what may surprise some people is that injecting 600 mg of testosterone per week led to a greater increase in muscle mass without any resistance training than resistance training naturally did. Of course, if more of an optimized training routine were provided, the results may have favored the trained and natural group more. However, based on this study alone, what we can say is that testosterone really works. However, just look at the scale of increase we're talking about here. At baseline, the concentrations were just around 500. And after 10 weeks of weekly injections, they were up around 3,000 nanogs per deciliter. That's an enormous increase, magnitudes greater than the acute changes you see from altering training methods, which only last acutely anyway. But what I really wanted to know was, would say, 800 make any difference? Or better still, would altering these levels anywhere within the natural normal physiological range help me get more jacked? And after scouring the literature on this, the best answer I can give is maybe. It seems really likely that increasing a low testosterone level of say around 300 NOGS per deciliter or lower to a more average level like say 600 NOGS per deciliter might help. Boston and colleagues were able to show that even in the absence of weight training, increasing testosterone levels from 306 to 570 nanogs per deciliter across 20 weeks led to a pretty big increase in fat-free mass as measured by hydrostatic weighing and dexa of about 3 kg or about 6 1/2 lb. So going from a hypogonatic level of 300 or less to a more normal range would almost certainly help. However, it's unclear whether or not going from a more middle of normal range to a more high end of normal range would do much. So, since we've already debunked the idea that acute increases around the training session does much of anything, the next thing I wanted to figure out was if there are other ways I could increase my testosterone naturally. And since we're on the topic of training, it does seem that overtraining is a bad idea. To give you some idea, overtraining in the literature has been defined as any increase in training volume and/or intensity resulting in long-term performance detriments. And it turns out that if you overdo it too much for too long, testosterone drops off. And overdoing training volume is worse for testosterone than overdoing training intensity. A study by Fryatal showed that doing 10 one rep max squats every day for two weeks didn't change testosterone concentrations. However, multiple studies have found that large increases in volume reduces testosterone. And such is the case when combining weight training with endurance training. According to the most recent work by Jones Ital, it seems that combining strength and endurance training in the same session, three days per week for six weeks, doesn't significantly decrease testosterone compared to strength training alone. However, this is in contrast to earlier research that did report a testosterone decrease when combining weights and cardio. So, it seems that if you're simply doing too much training volume when combining the two, then physiological stress will increase to a point past which endocrine changes favor catabolism, namely a dip in testosterone and a spike in cortisol. So to reduce this effect, try to stick to lower intensity cardio performed at a low to moderate frequency with shorter durations as much as you can, assuming your primary goal is muscle and strength gain. So what about diet? Isn't soy really bad for testosterone levels? Not really. The most comprehensive meta analysis on this topic, which analyzed 32 studies, came to the general conclusion that neither soy foods nor islavone supplements alter measures of bioavailable testosterone concentrations in men. For something more tangible,examine.com states that one to two servings of soy food daily and less than 25 grams of soy protein from non-conentrate sources doesn't impact testosterone and only seems to adversely affect testosterone when super loaded. And soy protein concentrate, even when highly consumed, probably doesn't affect testosterone because of its negligible isoflavone content, which are the compounds in soy that bind to the estrogen receptors to begin with. But more to the point, it seems that the main dietary factors driving changes in testosterone are body composition and caloric intake. In a 12-month case study following a natural bodybuilder during contest prep, it was found that as his caloric intake went from 4,750 calories to 2100 calories per day, taking his body fat from 14.8 to just 4.5%, his testosterone plummeted from 991 NOG per deciliter to just 227 NOG per deciliter. That's a 77% decrease. Rapid weight loss can also decrease testosterone. A 2008 study on competitive wrestlers found that just two to three weeks of caloric deficit caused a pretty huge 62% decrease in testosterone when caloric intake was limited to 500 to 1,000 calories per day. On the flip side, since overweight individuals tend to have lower testosterone and chronic overfeeding has been shown to reduce testosterone, there seems to be a sweet spot in terms of body fatness and caloric intake that will be specific to the individual. But by my estimation, sitting somewhere between 10 to 15% body fat will be a healthy spot to optimize testosterone levels for most men. The macronutrient composition of the diet also seems to matter with highfat diets generally outperforming low-fat diets. However, it seems that total energy intake is likely the main driver of these changes. And I personally think that as long as your fats are somewhere in the normal range of say 20 to 40% of caloric intake, testosterone levels can be optimized. There's also a slight trend towards saturated fat increasing testosterone more than other fats. So, including some saturated fat and sufficient fat generally may help in optimizing testosterone. However, keep in mind that the magnitude of these changes are relatively small, with one study showing a low-fat diet to reduce testosterone by 12% in older men and another study showing a higher fat diet to increase testosterone by 13% in young men. The vitamin and mineral composition of the diet also matters, but more so in the case of deficiencies. For example, a zinc deficiency can lower testosterone, so be sure to eat plenty of foods rich in zinc, like meat, shellfish, beans, and certain nuts. Vitamin D can also regulate testosterone levels. So depending on how much sun exposure you get, supplementation of up to 3,000 IU per day can increase testosterone. There are other supplements that generally have weak evidence supporting them, such as daspartic acid, but it only seems to work for 6 to 12 days before levels just go back to normal, and it isn't associated with any body composition changes. Ashwagandha has one study showing a 15% testosterone increase in young, healthy men, but the evidence is otherwise non-existent. Other supplements like tribulus, horny goat and fenugreek have no evidence, no human evidence and conflicting evidence respectively. For these reasons, I wouldn't focus on natural supplementation for boosting testosterone. So what about lifestyle factors like sleep, stress, and sex? While the correlation is not as strong as I would have expected, decreased sleep has been associated with decreased testosterone. While one 2011 study found that reducing sleep from 8 hours to 5 hours per night for 5 days reduced testosterone by 10.4% 4% in young men. A later 2012 study found that reducing sleep from 10 hours to 4 hours for 5 days noted only a trend for reduced testosterone that didn't reach statistical significance. On the other hand, sleep deprivation for 33 hours straight resulted in a 27% average drop in testosterone in 24 young men. Sexual activity seems to play an important role in testosterone regulation. One study published in the Journal of Evolution and Human Behavior showed that simply having a 5-minute conversation with an attractive woman was able to boost testosterone by 30% in men. Other research has shown that sexual arousal from viewing pornographic films can increase testosterone secretion in men, with one study showing that watching a 4-minute erotic video clip before training significantly improved three rep max strength on the squat. The quote freshness of one's relationship also seems to matter with a 2015 paper showing that single men and men in a new relationship which was defined as lasting less than 12 months had significantly higher testosterone than men in long-term relationships. And other earlier research showed that both men and women with polyamorous relationships or who have multiple committed relationships have higher testosterone than single folks with singly partnered individuals having the lowest testosterone of all. Of course, it's important to keep in mind before you dump your main squeeze or pick up a new one that we're simply looking at correlations. It seems to me that it could be the case that men and women with higher testosterone are just more likely to want or to be in multiple relationships, not necessarily that having multiple partners increases testosterone. While that remains a strong possibility given the variety and lifestyle preferences on this front, perhaps the most generalizable advice is to have regular sex since testosterone increases after evening quitis in both men and women. While on evenings with no intercourse, testosterone decreases as the night goes by. And for the record, ejaculation by itself isn't associated with changes in testosterone since the actual ejaculatory process is regulated by serotonin and nitric oxide, not testosterone. Other things worth mentioning that can optimize testosterone levels include de-stressing as much as possible, such as by meditating and relaxing and avoiding excessive alcohol intake, which has been shown to reduce testosterone. And so, while barring exogenous hormonal supplementation, all of these methods may not be enough to drastically improve your gains on their own, perhaps combining several of the actions discussed could make a significant enough impact on your gym performance and physique to make it worthwhile. mTLMEuDwab8.description: In the first lesson of our affiliate marketing course for beginners, you'll learn what affiliate marketing is and how it works.
mTLMEuDwab8.transcript: Hey, it’s Sam Oh and welcome you to our affiliate marketing course for beginners. This course is made up of 16 lessons and will walk you through the process, tactics, and strategies to create your own revenue-generating affiliate site. There’s no fluff, no bs, and no sales pitches for a premium course. So let’s get started with the first lesson, where we’ll talk about what affiliate marketing is and how it works. So affiliate marketing is when you promote another company's products. And when someone makes a purchase because of your referral, you're compensated with a commission. Now, in order for the affiliate company to attribute sales to the right person, the merchant usually uses a unique link that looks something like this, which is called an affiliate link. When a visitor clicks that link from your blog, website, or wherever, a cookie is stored on their device. And an affiliate cookie does two main things. First, it helps the merchant attribute the sale back to the right person. And second, cookies usually hold an expiration date, meaning people don't need to immediately purchase the product after clicking your link. Let's run through an example, so everything is crystal-clear. Let's say a visitor comes to your post about gardening hoses and clicks on some of your affiliate links. This leads them to a product on Amazon. But wait... they have to go and pick up their kids from soccer practice. So they do that, have dinner, and then go back to Amazon.com in their web browser. They find the product again and then purchase it along with a game console controller. Because they clicked on your affiliate link, a cookie was stored on their device. And because Amazon has a 24-hour cookie duration, you would still get compensated a percentage for both the garden hose and the console controller, even though you weren't promoting it. Now that we've covered the basics of what affiliate marketing is and how it works, let's talk about what you'll need to get started with affiliate marketing which is coming up in the next lesson.

Mas antes de eu vou ver, antes de responder, eu vou ver se você tá inscrito no nosso canal, tá? Eu vou responder igual, mas eu gostaria muito que você seguisse aí o nosso canal e divulgasse ainda mais para outras pessoas para estar conhecendo aqui eh esse movimento que é a nutrição celular de verdade, que é o emagrecimento, não a base de qualquer coisa, mas a base de nutrição e saúde de verdade. Então, um beijo para vocês, obrigada por ficar aqui com a gente até o final e até breve. M.

S4Hzad_zSAE.title: WEIGHT LOSS SUPPLEMENTS: What REALLY WORKS (and What is Pure Marketing)
S4Hzad_zSAE.description: 🟢 Discover the Weight Loss School, the online program designed for those who want to lose weight and never gain it back: https ...
S4Hzad_zSAE.transcript: Hoje você vai aprender quais suplementos realmente ajudam a emagrecer, quais não servem para nada e você só tá jogando seu dinheiro fora e quais podem ser perigosos pra sua saúde. Eu vou falar sobre todos os suplementos mais populares da atualidade: protein, creatina, colágeno, ômega-3, glutamina. E eu vou classificar tudo isso entre os úteis, os inúteis e os que fazem mal. Hoje em dia, o que mais tem são coisas com promessas de que ajuda a emagrecer e a maioria são promessas falsas. Mas curiosamente os que podem ajudar em alguma coisa são deixados de lado, as pessoas nem conhecem. Antes de mais nada, me diga aqui nos comentários se você usa algum suplemento e por que eu vou tentar ler e responder todos. Vamos nessa. Meu nome é Felipe Cageano. Eu sou nutricionista e eu defendo aquela nutrição baseada em evidência com respaldo científico. Não essa coisa louca de internet, de tomar um monte de suplemento, de fazer coisa detox ou essas modas radicais. Eu acredito que você consegue emagrecer só mudando a alimentação, sem passar fome e sem precisar cortar o que você gosta. Eu atendo presencialmente em São Paulo capital e online pro mundo inteiro. Eu também sou criador da escola de emagrecimento, onde mais do que uma dieta você aprende a comer, aprende a escolher e por causa disso você consegue manter o peso perdido. E eu uso as redes sociais para passar essas ideias e mostrar que o emagrecimento pode ser mais simples do que parece. Se isso faz sentido para você, já se inscreve no canal para assistir os próximos vídeos ou ver os vídeos antigos. Bom, hoje em dia os suplementos estão na moda. Todo ano aparecem novas opções, versões mais caras e o valor de mercado dessa indústria já ultrapassa R 1 trilhão deais no mundo. Então vamos parar de acreditar que suplemento é uma alternativa natural aos medicamentos e entender que sim, é uma grande indústria, sendo que a maioria dos fabricantes de remédios também produzem suplementos. Então a gente precisa de senso crítico e entender que o principal objetivo deles é ter lucro. E boa parte, para não dizer a grande maioria das alegações e promessa dos suplementos não tem respaldo científico. Ué, Felipe, mas se não tem, eles podem alegar. Enquanto a indústria de medicamentos é extremamente regulada, você precisa de muitos testes, experimentos e estudos para lançar um remédio no mercado. Para um suplemento é muito mais tranquilo, inclusive na hora de fazer essas alegações. Afinal, é só um suplemento. E junta-se isso, a ânsia das pessoas de encontrarem uma pílula mágica, uma coisinha que elas podem tomar para ter mais energia, mais disposição, para ganhar músculo, para perder gordura. e profissionais da saúde que enxergam isso com uma forma mais de lucrar e pronto, temos essa coisa dos tempos atuais de um monte de fórmula e prescrição de suplementos de forma indiscriminada. Não é incomum eu atender pacientes que estavam gastando mais de R$ 1.000 por mês em formulação de suplemento, sendo que não estavam vendo resultado nenhum e não havia motivo ou evidência para aquele tipo de prescrição. Então, eu acredito que a minha lista vai surpreender muita gente. Primeiro, eu vou falar para vocês quais não funcionam. Depois eu vou falar os que podem fazer mal. Tem que tomar cuidado. Às vezes você pode estar tomando um negócio que vai te causar algo muito sério. E por último, eu vou te dizer quais realmente funcionam pro objetivo de emagrecimento. Começando a lista dos que não funcionam, eu quero falar um que tá muito popular, os probióticos. São suplementos pensados para melhorar a microbiota intestinal. Afinal, os probióticos são bactérias que vão entrar no seu corpo, ir pro seu intestino e melhorar sua população de bactérias. Com isso vai melhorar sua saúde intestinal e por causa disso você vai emagrecer. Olha só que lindo. O problema disso é que não existe sequer estudos que mostram que probióticos vão de fato melhorar a microbiota e muito menos que tomar um probiótico vai levar você a emagrecer. Eu sei que na internet tá cheio de propaganda falando que você não emagrece por causa da microbiota. Tem bactérias que te impedem de emagrecer e você precisa do probiótico ideal ou qualquer coisa do tipo para queimar sua gordura. Balela. O que acontece é o seguinte: todas as pessoas têm bilhões e bilhões de bactérias no intestino. Esses probióticos t bactérias diferentes que supostamente são bactérias boas. E aí você vai ingerir e vai interagir com seu intestino, em tese vai melhorar. Só que o que a gente tá vendo na prática é que você não sabe o que vai acontecer quando ingere um probiótico, porque cada microbiota é diferente. E se antes parece que já tava se achando o que que era a microbiota ideal? Hoje a gente sabe que pessoas saudáveis têm microbiotas diferentes. Então quando você ingere um probiótico, pode acontecer qualquer coisa lá dentro e ninguém tem essa certeza de saber que realmente vai ajudar. E mesmo que ajudasse também não tem evidência que isso ajuda a emagrecer. É tudo muito teórico ainda, tudo tá sendo muito estudado. Eu acho que talvez daqui 10, 20, 30 anos a gente saiba de uma forma mais concreta como melhorar a nossa microbiota. Por hora, o que a gente tem de sólido é aumentar o consumo de fibras através de vegetais, legumes e frutas e consumir alimentos fermentados como queijos e iogurtes. Isso parece ser benéfico pra microbiota da maioria das pessoas. Esse artigo de revisão de 2019 diz claramente que existe muito mais um marketing do que evidência em relação ao uso de probióticos. E ano passado eu fui convidado pela Unicamp para fazer um curso fechado de dois dias junto com mais outros 15 ou 16 nutricionistas do Brasil. A gente foi selecionado e uma das aulas era de um dos maiores pesquisadores de microbiota aqui do Brasil, Dr. Mário Saad. E ele enfatizou que ele não recomenda o uso de probióticos. Isso pensando simplesmente em melhorar ali a saúde intestinal. Inclusive, ele mostrou estudos que o uso de probiótico não ajudou no diabetes, por exemplo. Enfim, muito cuidado. E vamos continuar falando de suplementos que não tem nada a ver com o emagrecimento, mas hoje em dia parece que a gente já trela qualquer coisa ao emagrecimento. Ômega-3, glutamina e colágeno. O ômega-3 é um suplemento com uma fama muito boa para diversos benefícios na saúde. Eu diria que a evidência tá longe de ser concreta, mas vamos focar aqui no emagrecimento. Esse aqui é um estudo muito legal, que pegou um grupo de pessoas e colocou essas pessoas para fazer dieta e exercício, só que metade delas ia tomar ômega3 e metade não ia tomar ômega-3. Sabe qual foi o resultado? Ambos os grupos emagreceram a mesma coisa. Quem tomou o ômega-3 não emagreceu mais. Então tomar ômega-3 para emagrecer não vai ajudar em nada. Ponto. Mas de repente quiser comer mais peixe para ingerir mais ômega-3, aí sim talvez ajude a emagrecer. Já a glutamina, sequer existe um estudo que a valice ela ajuda no emagrecimento, porque assim, não tem a mínima plausibilidade, não tem lógica, porque que o consumo de glutamina ajudaria alguém a emagrecer. Então, se você tá tomando por esse motivo, pode parar que não faz sentido. Já o colágeno, eu sei que muita gente toma por outros motivos, pele, cabelo, etc. A evidência também é fraca para isso, mas tem um estudo que comparou o uso do colágeno com o uso de outro suplemento que eu vou citar ainda nesse vídeo. E o que aconteceu? Quem usou esse outro suplemento perdeu mais gordura e ganhou mais massa muscular. Já quem usou o colágeno não perdeu nada de gordura. Então ele não é um suplemento que vai te ajudar no emagrecimento e mesmo não é uma boa fonte de proteína, tá? A composição do colágeno em termos de aminoácido é extremamente pobre. Eu, na minha prática, não recomendo colágeno pr ninguém. Agora, a gente tem suplementos que realmente são voltados para o emagrecimento. Eles são vendidos como isso é a principal função. E eu quero falar de dois deles, elarnitina e morosil. A elarnitina é uma molécula natural que é produzida pelo nosso fígado e os rins. E ela também pode ser obtida através da alimentação com carne, ovos, peixes e laticínios. E ela tem uma função no nosso corpo de transportar gordura. E a promessa é que se você tomar esse suplemento, vai te ajudar nesse transporte da gordura e seu corpo vai funcionar melhor, você vai ter mais energia e vai perder mais gordura. Agora, na prática, o que esse estudo mostrou foi que o pessoal que tomou a L carnitina perdeu 1 kg a mais. Só que olha o ponto importante, não perdeu nada de gordura, nem de medidas. Ou seja, o que perdeu de peso foi de líquido e não de gordura. Então se eles podem alegar, a carnitina faz perder peso, bem pouquinho, e não é gordura, que é o que a gente quer. Então a gente também não tem estudos que mostrem que tomar esse suplemento vai fazer você perder gordura. Já o morosil feito através do extrato da laranja muro, essa coisa bem natural que a galera gosta. E aqui a gente tem um problema. Os únicos estudos que a gente tem foram realizados pelos fabricantes do morosiu, ou seja, um grande conflito de interesse. E mesmo nesses estudos, os benefícios são muito pequenos. Só que o problema é que a lógica de como isso funciona não faz sentido. Não tem alguma coisa que você tome queima sua gordura. Os remédios de emagrecer, eles não queimam gordura. Não tem remédio que faz isso. O remédio de emagrecer não mexe nada no seu metabolismo. Remédio de emagrecer faz você ter menos apetite e por ter menos apetite você come menos e entra no déficit calórico e emagrece. Então, além de a gente não ter nenhum estudo confiável, a lógica por trás desse tipo de suplemento não faz sentido. Eu já recebi muitos relatos de pacientes e seguidores falando que usaram o Morosio e não viram resultado nenhum. Eu não recomendo. E falando em suplemento inútil, eu não podia deixar de citar talvez os dois mais inúteis de todos, BCAA e multivitamínicos. Eu não sei se realmente alguém acredita que usar eles vai ajudar a emagrecer, mas a questão é que eles não servem para nada. tá cheio de estudo mostrando que são dois suplementos que são perda de dinheiro. Multivitamínico não compensa a falta de vitaminas que você tem porque ele tem pouquinho de cada e não tem estudos mostrando que quem toma fica menos doente, vive mais ou qualquer coisa assim. É até o contrário. Tem estudo ali apontando que quem usa multivitamínico em geral tem uma expectativa de vida menor. Eu tenho até um vídeo sobre isso, que é esse aqui. TnPMIFHWuXw.title: O MELHOR suplemento PARA EMAGRECER? Mesmo sem fazer exercícios? Como usar?
TnPMIFHWuXw.description: Você já ouviu falar que existe um suplemento que pode ajudar no emagrecimento mesmo sem exercícios? Neste vídeo, eu ...
TnPMIFHWuXw.transcript: Olá, tudo bom? Hoje eu vou falar para você de um suplemento alimentar que é tido por muitas pessoas como melhor para emagrecer e mesmo sem exercício físico, eu vou te falar por que ele é tido dessa forma como um super aliado pro emagrecimento, como usar da forma correta e quais são os benefícios reais que ele traz. Você vai adorar. Fica até o final que você não vai querer perder. Antes de começar, eu quero te perguntar quantos anos você tem, quanto você tá pesando atualmente e se você tá buscando emagrecer ou não. Se você pudesse hoje decidir quanto você gostaria de perder de peso, quanto seria? Escreve para mim aqui embaixo nos comentários que eu vou adorar ver o seu comentário. Muitas pessoas sempre me perguntam: "Patrícia, me fala suplemento para emagrecer, quero alguma dica bem legal que tenha respaldo científico?" E justamente esse suplemento é um suplemento que cada vez mais vem tendo comprovação científica, que de fato pode ajudar bastante a emagrecer. Inclusive algumas pessoas têm tido ele como o melhor suplemento para emagrecer. Esse suplemento é o Protein e é um suplemento à base de proteína, ou seja, ele é a proteína do soro do leite. É uma proteína de alto valor biológico, com muitos componentes interessantes pra nossa saúde e que de fato pode trazer muitos benefícios no processo de emagrecimento. Uma das coisas que o IPO atende já traz logo é a saciedade, porque ele é proteína pura. a gente sabe que a proteína leva mais tempo lá no nosso estômago para ser digerida, para ser transferida, né, do estômago pro intestino e do intestino pra corrente sanguínea e que quanto mais tempo os alimentos ficam ali no no estômago, mais saciedade a gente tem, né, um sinal que vai ser montado para nossa pela distensão do estômago de que a gente tá saciado, que não precisa comer mais. Então, nessa forma indireta, ele ajuda a diminuir o hormônio da fome, que é a grelina, e aumenta o hormônio da saciedade, que é o GLP. Outro detalhe interessante é que o consumo de proteína aumenta o nosso gasto energético, né? A digestão da própria proteína consome mais calorias do que o carboidrato, por exemplo. Então, quando a gente tem um efeito termogênico do alimento, todo alimento tem esse efeito termogênico, né? que a gente tem a quantidade de calorias que são gastas pelas células do nosso corpo para conseguir pegar aquela energia e transferir, né, para dentro da corrente sanguínea. O efeito térmico da proteína é um efeito térmico bem mais considerável e por isso também pode ajudar no processo de emagrecimento de uma forma indireta. Mas basicamente o que mais ajuda no na questão do Protein é o fato dele preservar e manter massa magra. Tem muitas pessoas que estão em dieta, tem déficit calórico, em regime intermitente e tem muita perda de massa muscular. Aí os estudos já verificaram que as pessoas que consomem o prote, né, fazendo, perfazendo o comporto ideal de proteínas por dia, que pode ser entre 1 e 2 g de proteína por kilo de peso, pode ajudar a preservar a massa muscular e isso evita efeito sanfona depois. Isso evita que você perca muita água, perca muito músculo e depois quando você volta minimamente aquela alimentação que você tinha antes, você recupera muito rapidamente. Então manter a massa magra no processo de emagrecimento é muito fundamental pra nossa saúde e para manter esse emagrecimento depois por mais tempo, né, sem esse efeito sanfona, consumir o protein vai te ajudar nesse sentido também. E tem uma questão muito interessante, né, que muitas pessoas não sabem, mas o rei protein pode te ajudar mesmo sem exercício físico. Estudos verificaram que pessoas sedentárias quando incluem uma proteína dieta perdem mais gordura do que as pessoas sedentári que sedentárias que não suplementam o proteína na dieta. Então isso acontece porque os mecanismos de saciedade, o gasto calórico, efeito térmico do alimento que eu te falei, preservação de massa muscular, vão agir de forma independente do treino. Isso quer dizer que você não precisa treinar se tá querendo emagrecer? Não, porque se você aliar o uso do E protein, uma dieta equilibrada e a o treino, né, calórico, o gasto calórico aumentado, vai otimizar ainda mais esse processo e você vai ter resultados mais rápidos e mais sustentáveis. Então isso não tá querendo, eu não tô querendo dizer em momento nenhum aqui que o gasto calórico, o exercício físico, não tem um papel importante no emagrecimento. Super tem. E não só pro emagrecimento, né? O exercício físico é muito importante paraa nossa saúde. Controle da pressão, controle metabólico, bem-estar mental, saúde intestinal para tudo. Mas às vezes você tá passando por um momento em que você tá com alguma contraindicação médica para fazer exercício físico, já saiba que mesmo nesse caso você pode ter um efeito benéfico ao consumir o protein dentro dos limites ideais do seu dia. Então, quanto consumir de protein? Isso vai depender muito do restante da sua alimentação. Quando a gente pensa em proteína, a gente pensa, como eu te falei antes, né, em 1 g a 2 g de proteína por kg de peso. Então, se você tem 60 kg, de 60 a 120 g de proteína, mas não vão ser 120 g ou 60 g de protein. Você, a proteína tá presente no leite, no ovo, no iogurte, no frango, na carne, no peixe, nos feijões, nas leguminosas. Então tudo isso vai ter que ser levado em consideração do que você já consome e aí você pode acrescentar uma ou duas doses de bipotem 20 a 40 g por dia, dependendo das suas necessidades nível de atividade física, questões particulares. Lembra sempre que o excesso de proteína também é deletério para nosso corpo. Eu tenho vídeo falando sobre isso aqui também. Me conta aqui embaixo nos comentários, então, se você utiliza o Protein para emagrecer, se você vê uma melhora nessa questão da saciedade, nessa questão de nível de massa muscular do seu corpo, se você gosta dele pela praticidade, né? Muitas pessoas que não gostam de jantar, gostam de fazer um shake com ele à noite ou misturar no iogurte para ter uma alimentação equilibrada, com muita saciedade, sem pesar tanto no estômago, escreve para mim por que ele se encaixa bem na sua rotina e quais foram os resultados que você teve com e eu vou adorar ver o seu resultado. Lembra de deixar a curtida no vídeo, porque dessa forma você também ajuda. E compartilha esse vídeo aqui com quem você sabe que vai gostar, com quem precisa dessas dicas bacanas. Vamos juntos você ampliar essa corrente do bem que é isso que eu quero aqui com o canal. Até a próxima. Ciao. Ciao.
