_26t3XFCTf4.title: 5 Daily Habits That Protect the Prostate After Age 60 | Urologist Explained
_26t3XFCTf4.description: What if the #1 habit protecting your prostate takes just 30 seconds, and yet 90% of men over 60 are doing the opposite every ...
_26t3XFCTf4.channel_name: Dr. Mika Hayashi | Men's Sexual Health
_26t3XFCTf4.transcript: What if the number one habit protecting your prostate takes 30 seconds and 90% of men over 60 are doing the exact opposite? Hello, gentlemen. I'm Dr. Mika Hayashi, a urologist with over a decade of experience helping men over 60 avoid prostate surgery and reclaim their quality of life. Today I'm revealing five daily habits that can prevent 70% of prostate problems before they start. And I'm ranking them from surprising to absolutely gamechanging. Here's what shocked me. By age 70, over 80% of men have enlarged prostates. But research shows the men who practice these five specific habits experience 70% fewer symptoms. same age, completely different outcomes. The difference, five simple daily choices. Before we dive in, hit that like button right now. Comment below where you're watching from and subscribe so you never miss strategies that could save you from unnecessary suffering. And stick with me until habit number one because it's the most powerful thing you can do starting tonight. Now, let's go. Listen, your prostate is under attack every single day. By 60, your prostate has been producing fluid for four decades. It's been exposed to decades of inflammation, hormonal changes, poor circulation, and cellular damage. That's why benign prostatic hyperlasia, prostate enlargement affects over 50% of men by age 60 and jumps to 80% by age 70. The symptoms destroy your quality of life. Waking up three to five times nightly to urinate. Weak stream that takes forever to empty your bladder. Constant urgency making you plan your entire life around bathroom locations. Embarrassment avoiding travel or social events. But here's what most urologists won't tell you. This progression isn't inevitable. Research from Japan analyzing nationwide data found that healthy lifestyle habits can prevent or dramatically delay prostate problems. The five habits I'm sharing today target the actual mechanisms causing prostate damage. Chronic inflammation, poor circulation, hormonal imbalance, cellular oxidation, and mechanical pressure. One patient, 68 years old, was waking up six times nightly. His quality of life was terrible. After implementing these five habits for just 3 months, he was down to one nighttime trip. He told me, "I got my life back. I thought this was just what aging meant." These habits work. Now, let me show you exactly what they are. If you're ready to protect your prostate, hit that like button right now. Habit number five, walk 30 minutes daily. No excuses. Most men think walking is just for heart health. They're wrong. It's one of the most powerful prostate protectors. A 2024 study published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine tracked thousands of men. Those whose fitness improved by just 3% annually were 35% less likely to develop prostate cancer than men whose fitness declined. 35% from walking. Here's why this works. Walking reduces chronic inflammation throughout your body, including in your prostate. It improves blood circulation to your pelvic region, delivering oxygen and nutrients while removing waste. It helps you maintain healthy weight, and obesity directly accelerates prostate enlargement. Walking reduces stress hormones that aggravate prostate symptoms. Harvard tracked over 30,000 men for years. Those who walked regularly had significantly fewer prostate symptoms than sedentary men. Even moderate intensity walking yielded substantial benefits. The exact protocol 30 minutes daily every single day. One 30 minute walk or three 10-minute walks. Moderate pace where you can talk but feel your heart rate increase. No gym required, no special equipment. just consistent daily movement. One patient, 67 years old, had been completely sedentary. His prostate symptoms were severe. After committing to 30 minutes daily for 8 weeks, his nighttime bathroom trips dropped from 5 to 2. His stream improved noticeably. He reported feeling energized for the first time in years. Walking 30 minutes daily is non-negotiable, but the next four habits are even more powerful. Comment below, are you walking daily? Habit number four, limit alcohol to three drinks weekly maximum. This one surprises men because they think moderate drinking is fine. After 60, it's not. Research from Japan analyzing nationwide data showed that avoiding excessive alcohol consumption was critical for preventing prostate enlargement. But here's what they found. Even moderate drinking after 60 can aggravate prostate problems. Alcohol increases inflammation throughout your body, including your prostate. It disrupts hormone balance, suppressing testosterone and elevating estrogen. This hormonal chaos accelerates prostate growth. Alcohol exacerbates urinary symptoms, making urgency and frequency worse. The specific threshold is 3 to four drinks for the entire week. Not per day, for the whole week. Spread them out. One drink equals 12 o of beer, 5 o of wine, or 1.5 o of spirits. Never more than two drinks in one sitting. Ideally, one to two alcohol-free days between drinking. One patient was drinking four to five drinks nightly. His prostate symptoms were terrible. Constant urgency, weak stream, bathroom trips every hour at night. After cutting to three drinks weekly, his symptoms improved within 4 weeks. By 8 weeks, nighttime trips dropped from 6 to 2. He told me, "I thought alcohol helped me relax. It was destroying my prostate the entire time. Limit alcohol to three drinks weekly. Your prostate will respond within weeks. Stick with me because the top three habits are absolute game changers. Habit number three, eat cooked tomatoes three times weekly. This specific food has the strongest research evidence for prostate protection, and most men aren't eating nearly enough. Tomatoes contain lycopine, a powerful antioxidant that directly protects prostate cells from damage. But here's the critical part. Cooked tomatoes provide far more absorbable lycopine than raw. Studies show men who ate cooked tomatoes twice weekly had 28% lower risk of prostate cancer. Other research found higher lycopine intake was associated with reduced prostate enlargement and slower disease progression. 28% risk reduction from tomatoes. The exact protocol. Three servings of cooked tomatoes weekly. Tomato sauce on pasta. Marinara sauce. Tomato soup. Roasted tomatoes with olive oil. Cooking breaks down cell walls and makes lycopine bioavailable. Adding olive oil increases absorption even more. One serving equals half a cup of tomato sauce or one cup of cooked tomatoes. One patient incorporated tomato-based meals three times weekly, added cruciferous vegetables, and snacked on pumpkin seeds. After 6 months, his PSA levels dropped from borderline high to normal range. His urologist was impressed with improvements achieved through diet alone. Eat cooked tomatoes three times weekly. This is one of the most powerful dietary interventions for prostate health. But habits two and one are even more critical. Keep watching. Habit number two, maintain healthy weight. Lose 10% if overweight. Obesity doesn't just raise prostate cancer risk. It's associated with more aggressive disease and higher death rates. A 2022 study found direct connections between waist circumference, sedentary behavior, visceral belly fat, and enlarged prostate. The more belly fat you carry, the faster your prostate grows. Here's why. Excess body fat increases inflammation throughout your body. It disrupts hormones, raising estrogen and insulin while suppressing testosterone. This hormonal imbalance accelerates prostate enlargement and increases cancer risk. But here's the encouraging part. Losing just 10% of your body weight can significantly improve prostate health outcomes. The sustainable approach is portion control. Reduce portions by 20% to 25% at every meal. Use smaller plates. Stop eating when you're 80% full, not stuffed. Focus on vegetables, fruits, lean proteins, whole grains, healthy fats from nuts and fish. Limit red meat, processed foods, added sugars. Combine this with your daily 30inut walk. This creates sustainable weight loss without extreme dieting. One patient weighed 240 lb at 62. His prostate was significantly enlarged. Symptoms were severe. After losing 25 pounds over 6 months through portion control and walking, his symptoms improved dramatically. His urologist noted measurable reduction in prostate size on follow-up exam. Maintain healthy weight through portion control. If you're overweight, commit to losing 10%. Now, habit number one is the most powerful thing you can do starting tonight. This is the game changer. Hit that like button if you're still with me. Habit number one, never hold your urine. Respond immediately. This is the single fastest way to damage your prostate. And 90% of men over 60 do it multiple times daily without realizing the harm. Here's what happens when you hold urine. Your bladder stretches and presses directly on the prostate sitting below it, causing irritation and chronic swelling. Your bladder muscles weaken from constant over distension. Stagnant urine breeds bacteria and inflammation. Research confirms that timely voiding prevents bladder over distension and reduces pressure on the prostate, preventing long-term damage. But most men ignore this. They hold it during meetings, errands, driving, watching TV. Every single time you're damaging your prostate, the fix is immediate response. When you feel the urge to urinate, go immediately. Don't delay. Don't wait. Don't hold it. Plan your day around bathroom access. If you're traveling or in situations where bathrooms are limited, reduce fluid intake slightly ahead of time rather than holding urine for hours. One patient, 64 years old, routinely held urine for hours during work. He developed severe urgency and weak stream. After I explained the damage, and he started responding immediately to signals, his symptoms improved dramatically within 6 weeks, he told me. I had no idea holding it was destroying my prostate. This one simple change fixed everything. Never hold your urine. This single habit protects your prostate more than almost anything else. This is habit number one because it's the most powerful, the easiest to implement, and it starts working immediately. Comment below right now. Are you holding your urine daily? Let me give you the exact system to implement all five habits starting today. Tonight, immediately. Habit one. From this moment forward, respond immediately every time you feel urination urge. Never hold it. Starting tomorrow morning. Habit five, walk 30 minutes. Set a non-negotiable daily appointment with yourself. Habit two, reduce your breakfast portion by 20%. Use a smaller plate this week. Habit four, track your alcohol consumption honestly. Cut it to three drinks maximum for the entire week. Habit three, buy tomatoes, tomato sauce, or marinara. The truth is, these frustrating symptoms often have less to do with age and more to do with a small but powerful gland, your prostate. When it's inflamed, it presses on your bladder and urethra, disrupting sleep, slowing your flow, and even stealing your intimacy. I've spent over 30 years helping men reverse these issues naturally. And I can tell you, relief doesn't always start with a pill. In fact, the answer could be sitting quietly in your kitchen right now. Today, I'll reveal five powerful fruits that can help shrink your prostate, restore healthy circulation, and bring back the energy and confidence you thought you'd lost. One of them can start reducing inflammation in less than 18 hours. And chances are, you've eaten it your whole life without realizing its true power. Let's start this journey with fruit number five, a small green powerhouse that men often overlook. I'm Dr. Dr. Marissa Blake, a boardcertified urologist and men's health specialist. For more than three decades, I've worked with thousands of men from their early 40s to well into their 80s, helping them restore urinary comfort, improve circulation, and reclaim their confidence without relying solely on medication. My approach is simple. Treat the root cause, not just the symptoms. Over the years, I've seen firsthand how small Consistent changes like adding the right fruits to your daily routine can create powerful lasting improvements in a man's health and vitality. If you're over 60, you might know this routine all too well. You lie down at night, ready to rest, but within a couple of hours, you're awake again and again, making trip after trip to the bathroom. Each time your bladder feels urgent, but your stream is weak and slow. When you finally get back to bed, your mind races, and sleep feels impossible. By morning, you're tired before the day has even started. The fatigue makes it harder to stay focused, harder to enjoy hobbies, and harder to be fully present with family. For many men, these constant interruptions start to affect their mood, patience, and relationships. And then there's intimacy. When you're dealing with pelvic pressure, poor circulation, or the lingering embarrassment of frequent bathroom breaks, it's hard to feel confident in the bedroom. Some men quietly avoid physical closeness, fearing they won't perform the way they used to. I remember one patient, Dennis, who told me, "Doc, I feel like I'm slowly fading away. Not just physically, but as a man." He wasn't alone. Countless men share this silent frustration, thinking it's just age and that nothing can be done. The truth, in many cases, the real problem is an inflamed prostate. When the small gland swells, it presses on the urethra and bladder, creating that constant urge to urinate and the weak, hesitant flow. It can also restrict blood flow to the pelvic area, draining energy and dampening intimacy. Daily habits, diet, and low antioxidant intake quietly fuel this inflammation. Left unressed, it chips away at your comfort, confidence, and quality of life. But here's the good news. The prostate can respond remarkably well to the right kind of support. Natural, nutrient-rich foods, especially certain fruits can help reduce swelling, improve circulation, and restore that sense of ease you've been missing. And that's exactly what we're going to explore today. Before we talk about the five fruits, let's understand why these simple foods can make such a big difference and why your prostate depends on them more than you might think. To understand why these symptoms happen and how simple fruits can help, let's start with the prostate itself. Your prostate is a small gland about the size of a walnut located just below your bladder. Its main job is to produce fluid that nourishes and protects sperm. The urethra, the tube that carries urine out of the body, runs right through the middle of it. When you're young and healthy, the prostate is firm, smooth, and doesn't get in the way of urine flow. But over time, oxidative stress, hormonal changes, and lowgrade inflammation can cause it to swell. Here's the problem. Because the urethra passes through it, even a little swelling is like putting a kink in a garden hose. The flow slows, the pressure builds, and your bladder never feels fully empty. That's why you keep waking up at night and rushing to the bathroom, even when there's not much urine to pass. Inflammation is the main culprit. It's triggered by tiny molecules called free radicals, unstable compounds that damage your cells. They can come from poor diet, chronic stress, environmental toxins, and even normal aging. If your diet is low in antioxidants, the nutrients that neutralize these free radicals, the damage keeps building, and the swelling never fully goes down. Poor circulation makes it worse. _M7VhhptSrU.title: This Anti-Aging Supplement Might Actually Work
_M7VhhptSrU.description: Visit https://brilliant.org/scishow/ to get started learning STEM for free. The first 200 people will get 20% off their annual premium ...
_M7VhhptSrU.transcript: Who doesn’t want to live forever? Well, a lot of people I’m sure. Forever is…well… forever. But whether it’s for our health or our vanity, most of us probably wouldn’t say “no” to a long life without the side effects that come with getting older. And if you’re searching for ways to stay young, you may have stumbled upon a new kind of anti-aging supplement: one based on a molecule called nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide. NAD+ for short. And while you shouldn’t spend your life’s savings on the stuff, it turns out that science might be onto something here. Might. [♪ INTRO] Human bodies age because they accumulate cellular damage over time. This damage can come from a variety of sources. Some are external factors, like UV radiation and pollution. Some are internal, like the genetic errors that slowly build up as our cells copy their DNA over and over to make more of themselves. So over time, our cells can begin to malfunction, or they simply die without replicating, leaving no replacement. So aging is a gradual decline in function. And with it comes a higher risk of age-related illnesses like heart disease, cancer, and neurodegenerative disorders. Modern medicine has gotten better at treating many of these diseases, but hasn’t worked out how to stop aging in the first place. At least not yet. But this is where NAD⁺ enters the chat. Our bodies are constantly making NAD⁺ throughout our lives, and it’s what scientists call a coenzyme, a molecule that helps enzymes do their jobs in the cell. And this particular coenzyme supports several essential functions in every cell of your body. NAD⁺ plays a major part in DNA repair, inflammation reduction, and keeping your mitochondria working properly. But as we get older, the amount of NAD⁺ in our bodies drops. It’s a totally natural process, but this decline contributes to the cellular damage and breakdown we see as aging. So scientists have a hypothesis: that boosting a person’s NAD⁺ levels could be the key to combating the aging process. And like all good scientists, they’ve been testing that hypothesis. Some researchers have focused on supplements that don’t specifically contain NAD⁺, but one of two smaller molecules that our bodies can turn into NAD⁺. Their names are both mouthfuls, so we’ll just call them NR and NMN. And that’s because NAD⁺ is a lot harder for your body to absorb. It has to be injected directly into your bloodstream, but the precursor molecules can be taken in pill form. So even if literally no one on the planet was afraid of needles, those pills would be a much cheaper anti-aging intervention, assuming the research proves the hypothesis correct. And the results of these supplements have been promising… at least if you’re a lab animal. And if you’re a human animal, you might be interested in these sponsors. Thanks to Brilliant for supporting this SciShow video! One of the best things about SciShow and Brilliant is that you can access them anywhere with an internet connection. Brilliant makes it easy to learn right on your phone with lessons you can complete whenever and wherever you feel like it. And that’s more often than you might think, because Brilliant’s lessons are just minutes long. So you can finish one up while you’re in the grocery checkout line or waiting for your doctor’s appointment or before your friend meets you for coffee. You can learn on the go at Brilliant.org/SciShow, the QR code on the screen, or the link in the description. You’ll get 20% off an annual premium Brilliant subscription and the first 30 days for free. In one paper from 2013, a team of scientists gave some geriatric mice daily doses of NMN for one week, and the mice wound up showing better muscle function. Researchers have also carried out studies on tiny worms, called C. elegans. Testing on worms might sound a bit pointless, but these little creatures are actually pretty similar to us, biologically speaking. We share many of the same genes and bodily processes, so scientists use them as sort of “simplified” versions of the human body, to see what side effects certain drugs might have on us. Both positive and negative. And increasing NAD⁺ levels in C. elegans worms, either by giving them those precursor supplements, or by genetically engineering them to maintain higher NAD⁺ levels in their bodies, appears to extend their life and delay certain age-related declines. But, despite all their similarities, mice and worms are not humans. So, the big question remains, does this promising research translate to us? So far, the results in human trials have been encouraging, but limited. One study in middle-aged and older adults showed that taking NR supplements increased NAD⁺ levels without any significant side effects, and might have some potential for treating heart disease. Another trial, which used NMN instead, was in postmenopausal women with pre-diabetes. The women who took the supplement had better insulin sensitivity in their muscles compared to women who took a placebo. And back in 2022, a research team published a big analysis that looked at multiple studies to see what we could learn from all of these findings put together. They found that the supplements may help manage your cholesterol. And our cholesterol levels can get harder to manage as we age, although that’s mostly because of how our lifestyles change as we age, not the aging itself. Either way, I have to say “may” because the decrease they found wasn’t statistically significant. But in more positive news, it appeared that increasing NAD⁺ can reduce certain chemical signals associated with inflammation. That’s good because chronic inflammation is linked to a wide range of diseases and health issues. But while these studies show that boosting NAD⁺ in our aging bodies can do us some good, they fall short of the life-extension promises seen in worms. Of course, that hasn’t stopped the wellness industry from enthusiastically embracing NAD⁺ as an anti-aging miracle. You can wander down the aisle of your local drug store and pick up a bottle of non-FDA regulated NAD⁺ precursor right now. Or if you’ve got even more money to burn, you could do what Jennifer Aniston told The Wall Street Journal she does, and get hooked up to a NAD⁺ IV drip. Luxury wellness centers currently offer NAD⁺ infusion treatments that’ll set you back hundreds of dollars per session! The reality, however, is that we’re still in the early days of understanding the full impact of boosting NAD⁺ levels in humans. Whether or not NAD⁺ turns out to be our best bet against aging, what can we do to stave off the inevitable march of time? Well, instead of trying to expand our natural lifespan, we can try using well-established, if unglamourous techniques to maximize our healthspan, the number of years we live in good health, free from chronic disease. And yes, I mean the boring stuff, like eating a healthy diet, exercising regularly, not smoking, and so forth. Maybe just focus on the basics before splurging on NAD+ products. Boosting NAD⁺ certainly isn’t the magic bullet some companies are claiming it to be. But maybe, just maybe, we’ve found a fancier way to help us live longer, if not forever. [♪ OUTRO]

RODwPAdtrw8.title: The most exciting REAL anti-aging drugs
RODwPAdtrw8.description: Find out more about real anti-aging drugs in my book, _Ageless: The new science of getting older without getting old_ https ...
RODwPAdtrw8.transcript: Did you know that there are drugs that can slow down the aging process? This is a really big deal, and not just to avoid wrinkles and grey hair: it’s because our risk of diseases like cancer, heart disease and dementia all increase exponentially with age. So what we’re talking here is treatments that might be able to slow down, or even reverse the aging process itself, delaying many or even all of these diseases, plus frailty, sight, hearing and memory loss, and even that cosmetic stuff like wrinkles and grey hair, with a single pill. So, what kind of pills are we talking about here? There are lots of contenders, but I’ve narrowed things down to my top five real anti-aging drugs. Let’s start with one of the most-hyped longevity drugs: rapamycin. Rapamycin was first discovered in bacteria in the soil on Easter Island, which you might know as the one with the massive stone heads, and it’s known as Rapa Nui in the local Polynesian, so that’s where rapamycin got its name. It’s normally used to help transplant patients avoid rejection, and as a coating for stents, and as a cancer treatment. But work in the 2000s showed it might be able to do much more than that: it could slow down the aging process itself. The excitement around rapamycin really got going in 2009, when scientists working at the Interventions Testing Program, considered the gold standard for testing longevity drugs in mice, found that rapamycin extended mouse lifespan by 10%. And it’s not just mice – we’ve also got quite a bit of suggestive human data on rapamycin, from over two decades of transplant patients and other people using it, and also from some trials looking specifically at its use in aging. One trial of 650 older people showed that a similar drug improved their immunity, and that serious side-effects from this kind of treatment are rare. So, what are we waiting for?! Should we all be taking rapamycin? Well, unfortunately, although the evidence we have is mostly positive, we don’t have the perfect evidence: a nice big randomised clinical trial to tell us one way or the other whether it really improves general health in older people. But there is some good news: that work in mice showed us that rapamycin still works even if given to relatively old mice, roughly equivalent to 60 years old in human terms – so there’s no rush for most of us, we’ve got time to wait for that perfect experiment to come in, and tell us one way or the other whether rapamycin really is a longevity drug. Next up, some of the hottest new anti-aging treatments: drugs called ‘senolytics’. As we age, lots of things change in our bodies, and one of those is that we accumulate cells that are known as ‘senescent cells’. ‘Senescent’ is just the scientific word for ‘old’, and these are cells that have divided too many times, become damaged, and so on. But, not only do these senescent cells stop dividing, they also emit a toxic cocktail of molecules that can accelerate loads of aspects of the aging process. But the good news is that scientists have developed drugs called ‘senolytic’ drugs that can remove these old cells, while leaving the rest of the cells of the body intact. In 2018, scientists published a paper where they gave a senolytic cocktail to mice aged 24 months old, which is pretty old for a mouse, and the mice basically got biologically younger. They lived a few months longer, perhaps equivalent to a few years in human terms, but most importantly they stayed healthier: they delayed diseases like cancer and they could run further and faster on the tiny mouse-size treadmills they use in these experiments – a kind of mousey version of delayed frailty. And frankly, the mice just look fantastic – you don’t need to be an expert mouse biologist to guess which of these two mice got the drugs. The senolytic cocktail here is known as D+Q: it’s a mixture of dasatinib, a chemotherapy drug, but used at far smaller doses than you’d use it against cancer, plus Q, quercetin, a ‘flavanol’ molecule found in fruit and veg. These weren’t picked because they’re necessarily the best we could do, but because they’re drugs the scientists had on their shelves which, after testing a few dozen, were found to be the most effective at killing senescent cells in a dish. So, if we actively go out hunting for real senolytic drugs, I think we’ll be able to find much more effective ones than this first attempt. In fact, twenty or thirty companies are already trying to do just that, with a wide variety of different approaches. So senolytics certainly seem worth getting a little bit excited about. And that’s why they’re number two on my list of the most promising longevity drugs. Up third, diabetes drug, and maybe longevity pill, metformin. If you’ve read any books about aging biology, or seen much about longevity online, you’ve probably come across metformin: a diabetes drug that might do far more than keep people’s blood sugar under control. The most exciting results came from a study of medical records in the UK comparing diabetics taking metformin to a control group of patients without diabetes who therefore weren’t taking the drug. And they found that the people with diabetes outlived the control group: only by a few months but, given that diabetes usually comes bundled with other health problems, you’d normally expect people with it to survive less long than people without. So the question is, could the metformin have been helping not just with their diabetes, but with other age-related problems like cancer too? What we need is again a proper, randomised trial – and there’s a trial called TAME, or Targeting Aging with MEtformin, that hopes to enlist 3000 healthy older people, and hand out metformin to half of them but this time at random rather than just the ones who happen to go on to develop diabetes, and see whether those with metformin or without live longer. Frustratingly, it’s been ready to go for quite a few years now, but they can’t find the funding. So let’s hope that they find that money soon, and maybe TAME can be the first ever trial of a drug against aging itself. Number four is one I’m newly bullish about: taurine. If you’ve heard of taurine, it’s probably because it’s found in energy drinks like Red Bull and Monster. Or because of a huge study released in 2023 that found that it slowed aging in mice and monkeys. The study authors found that taurine levels decline with age in mice, monkeys and humans, and that topping up taurine with supplements could make the mice live 10% longer. They also tried giving supplements to monkeys and, though the monkeys did get healthier, it’ll be a few more years before we know if they lived longer too. I’m possibly running the risk of being a bit zeitgeist-y by including taurine because the study that caused all this excitement came out a few months ago at the time of recording. But, it is quite a solid result both in terms of mouse lifespan, and it’s unusual and pretty cool to have results in monkeys too. It was also a massive study, with 56 authors, representing more than a decade of work – both of which make me hope someone will grab the bull by the horns and run a human trial to find out if taurine can have health benefits for us too. And finally…statins? Statins are another very widely prescribed drug normally used to reduce levels of cholesterol in your blood, and prevent problems like heart attack and stroke. But could they be anti-aging drugs too? I think maybe they could, and the first reason is that their normal function of lowering cholesterol could be described as anti-aging, in a way. As time passes, we all accumulate fatty deposits in the walls of our blood vessels, and this process probably starts before we’re even born. While you might think of gummed-up arteries and heart attacks as something that happens in older people, pathologists have found the early stages of these so-called ‘atherosclerotic plaques’ in the blood vessels of teenagers who’ve died from completely unrelated causes like car crashes. Of course, most teenagers, or even most 50-somethings, won’t have accumulated enough of these plaques to cause them a heart attack or a stroke but, eventually, these build-ups can get so big that they can cause serious problems. One way or another, the build-up of cholesterol in the walls of our arteries is responsible for about a third of deaths, both in the rich countries and globally. So this does sound like a cumulative process that could be thought of as part of aging – and statins, by reducing your cholesterol levels, can slow aging down this way. But they might also have other anti-aging side-effects. As we age, immune cells called neutrophils lose their ability to navigate towards danger, and instead take random paths through our tissues, that can cause damage that may accelerate aging, and researchers have found that statins can restore these cells’ sense of direction. There’s also some evidence that statins might increase the length of telomeres – protective caps on the end of our DNA that get shorter as we get older – and statins might even be able to reduce the risk of cancer. A systematic review of 54 statin studies found that they reduced the risk of death from any cause by more than a quarter, which could correspond to maybe a few years of extra healthy life. This could be driven mostly or entirely by the reduction in the risk of heart disease – but it would be cool to see some more research to find out whether statins could have a more general anti-aging effect. All of which is to say: maybe the first anti-aging drugs are with us already? So, is it time to start popping longevity pills? Well, unless you’re advised to take one of these by your doctor for a specific condition, I think most of us would be best advised to watch and wait. We have loads of ideas to slow down or perhaps even reverse aging that work in the lab, be that in cells in a dish or in mice, but which exact drugs will be safest and most effective in humans can only be determined by some proper clinical trials. Given that we’ve not done these trials, and that there are literally hundreds of drugs which might have an effect on healthy lifespan, you might be wondering how I went about choosing a top five. Well, firstly, this isn’t a be-all-and-end-all list: new evidence or just a new day of the week could change what exactly makes it into my shortlist. But there are a few general things to look out for when trying to identify potential promising longevity drugs. Firstly, does it work robustly in mice? The first and best place to check is the Interventions Testing Program, who conduct probably the most rigorous possible tests of longevity drugs. And the ITP has given negative results for some proposed longevity drugs: for example, a lack of effect for drug called nicotinamide riboside, also known as NR, that supposedly boosts levels of something else called NAD and some people say might be a longevity drug, is one reason I’m not super-excited about NR, NMN or related compounds. But the ITP also found nothing when they tried a variety of other things which I am more excited about: things like metformin and a couple of statins, and there are some thing they haven’t tried yet, like taurine. So, what if there’s no results in mice? Are there results in other organisms commonly used in aging experiments like flies, worms or yeast, or indirect results in humans? Now, all of these normally come with pitfalls – for example, in human trials, we don’t give drugs for longevity yet so the results are usually for people with a particular disease, which means they might not generalise to healthy people who are just getting older. So what would make your top five longevity drugs, and are there any in particular you’d like me to look into? Let me know in the comments! And I’ll make an update to this video if there are any exciting new developments, so if you enjoyed it, please hit like, and also hit subscribe to be the first to hear when that comes out. In the meantime, check out more longevity science in this video, or you might enjoy my book, Ageless: The new science of getting older without getting old. Click here to find out more!

dqD4It8hJFg.title: I’m Changing How My Patients Do Anti-Aging Skincare (5 Key Upgrades)  | Dr. Sam Ellis
dqD4It8hJFg.description: Most anti-aging skincare routines are stuck in the past. In this video, I break down 5 smart skincare upgrades I'm making for my ...
dqD4It8hJFg.transcript: Every year, new skincare trends pop up. And as a dermatologist, I'm constantly asked, "Okay, but what's actually worth doing?" Well, today I'm sharing five things that I am implementing in my own clinical practice this year. These are all evidence-based upgrades that are going to help my patients get the best results, and hopefully you, too. If you're new here, hi, I'm Dr. Sam Ellis. I'm a board-certified medical and cosmetic dermatologist in Northern California, and I love helping you get the best skin of your life without wasting your time or money. So, if that sounds good to you, make sure you like this video and you're subscribed to the channel because I have so many good videos coming. All right, let's get into the first upgrade of the year, which is antioxidant optimization. I often will get asked by my patients or by random people when they find out I'm a dermatologist, do I need to be using vitamin C, and is vitamin C actually helpful? And generally I say yes, vitamin C is great. It is the most studied antioxidant when it comes to boosting collagen levels in the skin and fighting things like hyperpigmentation. And antioxidants matter and they are helpful because they help neutralize free radical damage from things like UV radiation, pollution, and inflammation, which are all things that prematurely age your skin. But vitamin C can be really irritating for some skin types, or in some individuals, it may cause them to break out. And so rather than saying, "Okay, never mind. I'm just not going to use an antioxidant. Let's talk about some alternatives. And in reality, there are so many options, but the vitamin C alternatives that I tend to recommend include resveratrol, nyioinamide, co-enzyme Q10, green tea, and definitely azelleic acid. These are all tried and true and have been used in the skincare space for a long time to boost the skin's defenses against external stressors and optimize the aging process. And I particularly lean into rveratrol and co-enzyme Q10 for those that are really sensitive or find that vitamin C irritates their skin. If you're not new to the channel, you have definitely heard me talk about rveratrol before. In my mind, it's very much a powerhouse antioxidant because it helps with calming, it supports collagen production, and it can help reduce redness, which is something I'm very focused on because I get red. If someone is looking for a great antioxidant product with rveratrol, I will often recommend the Allies of Skin Betagalucan and Rveratrol hydrating serum. Not only does this have rveratrol, but it's packed with other antioxidants as well. It has green and white tea extracts. It has superoxxide dismutates and it has carnosine, which is a peptide that has antioxidant effects. Plus, I sort of see this as a twoin-one product because it's a hydrating serum and an antioxidant. So, for those looking to streamline their routine or just get more bang for their buck with a product, I love it. And something like this can be used morning or night. There's a lot of misinformation out there that antioxidants are only effective when you use them during the day, but you can use them at night as well. So, wherever it fits best in your routine. And if you already use vitamin C and love it, you could always think about using vitamin C in the morning and a rveratrol product at night, especially if you're looking for that extra calming effect from your antioxidant. The brand Koti also has a really nice hydrating rveratrol serum that's a little richer than the one from Allies of Skin and you would use it the exact same way. The other antioxidant serum that I will often recommend especially if someone is on a tighter budget is the Timeless Co-Eme Q10 serum. This not only has co-enzyme Q10 but it also has metrical 3000 which is a great anti-aging peptide. So this is a powerhouse peptide and a powerhouse antioxidant in one very affordable serum. And again, with antioxidants, we are generally putting them quite close to the skin. So, usually after you cleanse in the morning or at night before you go in with any of your other serums or moisturizers or sunscreen. Now, speaking of tweaking your routine to fit your skin instead of forcing your skin into that one right thing, let's talk about the next thing I'm working on with my patients. 2026 is the year of customized retinoids. For so long, the messaging online was for the best anti-aging results possible, everyone should be using prescription tininoan. And just like vitamin C, for the right skin, tininoan is incredible. But it is not the only option. If you have sensitive skin or you are just starting your retinoid journey, you do not need to use tininoan to get amazing anti-aging benefits with that aspect of your skincare. Because one thing I was noticing with a lot of my skincare consults is they would come in, I'd be meeting that patient for the first time, and when I was looking at their skin up close, it was really flaky and their skin kind of seemed irritated. And when I would inquire about their skincare routine, they would tell me about how they're using treinoan a couple times a week or every single night. And even though I know they were pushing themselves to be able to tolerate it, it was very clear to me that their skin was unhappy with that product. And I know some patients are so dedicated to their tininoan and they just want to try and power through. But what I can tell you as a dermatologist is if you've been using tininoan consistently for more than 6 months and your skin is still unhappy, it's time to change it up. So, I've been talking a lot with my patients about starting low and slow with their retinoid. They are not failing their anti-aging skincare routine because they can't use treinoan. We use retinol or retinaldahhide instead and they still get amazing results. And in the office during my cosmetic consultations, I'm often talking to my patients about procedures that they can do and we get a little bit into skincare and the overarching goals, but then we separately have my patients meet with our skincare specialists. Shout out to Sierra, Anna, Michelle, Paris who can then help them sort of curate a really good routine and find the right ramp up routine for their retinoid and how to bring in their other skincare products so that they have a really strong skinincare regimen that is not irritating to their skin and is going to support any procedures or other things they do in the office. Now, of course, as a dermatologist, I do have some of my favorite retinols and retinaldahhide products that I often talk about with my patients. The skin better alpharet is one of my tippy top favorites. Right now, you can only get it in dermatology clinics or from an aesthetician and it is one of the pricier retinoids on the market, but when it comes to being spendy with skincare, it is one of the things that I think is truly worth the investment if you have really sensitive skin. If you want a clinically tested retinoid that has shown to be good for sensitive skin, yield really efficacious results, absolutely worth trying. You guys know I'm also a huge fan of Korean beauty products. And actually in my K beauty products of the year for 2025, I included the Haruharu Wonder Night Retinol Serum, which is a beautiful beginner retinol serum that is incredibly affordable. I'll put a link for it in the description box because when you are buying K beauty, you always want to make sure you are purchasing from a reputable source. You also may know that with my own brand, Prequel, we launched a retinol or retinaldahhide product last year. And when you think about retinoids, which is that broad category of vitamin A derived molecules that help with skin aging in a very expansive and comprehensive way, you have retinol, which gets converted into retinol or retinaldahhide in the skin, which then gets converted into retinoic acid, which is essentially tininoan and that's the active molecule that is creating all those beautiful changes. So when you are going from retinol to retinel, you get a product that is a little more potent and that works a little bit faster. And so if I have a patient who's doing really well on their retinol and they want to level it up, that's when we introduce retinaldahhide. And sometimes that's where my patients stop on their retinoid journey. They go from retinol to retinaldahhide. And that is the most potent thing that their skin can handle without getting overly irritated. And I always try to reiterate, it is so important when it comes to retinoids to focus on consistency and frequency of application over potency. I would much rather, as I think any dermatologist would, have you apply your retinoid five to seven nights a week that is not as strong than focus on something like tininoan and only be able to tolerate it two nights a week. Last year, I was just having so many new patients come into clinic who are so excited about skincare and kind of doing the most but stressing their skin out too much. And so that is why we started working with our skincare specialists in the office. So patients meet with me to talk about procedures and they meet with our specialists to talk about skincare and really tailoring a routine to meet their skin where it's at and help with their skincare goals. Moving on, another way I am rehauling my patients skin this year is placing a bigger focus on body skin. I treat men and women in my clinic, but I find that more often women notice these body skin changes and always it's been from pregnancy, general weight shifts, pmenopause, menopause, but I have so many more patients losing a lot more weight over the past years generally because of the rise of GLP-1 agonist, which is great. They are so stoked about what it's doing for their body, their confidence, their mood, how they feel, but they are not thrilled about what it is doing to their skin because they feel like their skin gets a lot crepier, saggier, and just overall they're just noticing the skin on their body way more than they ever did before. Now, I will say if you have significant sagging of your skin, face or body, surgery is the only thing that's going to make a really exciting difference. But if we are talking about crepey texture, dullness, roughness, early signs of laxity, a diligent body skincare routine can really make a difference. And often it's not about doing the same thing every single day for your body skin. I often have my patients kind of on a rotation of a few different things, but they are applying something to their body every single day to support the process. First and foremost, glycolic acid for crepiness and texture. Yes, glycolic acid is an exceptional exfoliant. So, it can make your skin very smooth and hydrated, but it can also help boost collagen levels within your skin. So, you guys know I love my prequel multi acid milk peel. It's 15% glycolic acid, which can be used on the face, but I think is even better utilized sometimes on the body after your shower a couple times a week. Applying that everywhere will make your skin so soft, so smooth. And when you do this consistently over the course of months, you will notice that your skin is more supple as well. So that's one way you can incorporate it. The brand Alpha Skincare also makes an amazing 12% glycolic acid body lotion. Uh Glyone also makes a great glycolic acid body lotion. So I will link all of those in the description box. It just kind of depends on where you want to incorporate and how you want to incorporate glycolic acid into your routine. One thing that's very important to note is that glycolic acid and all alpha hydroxy acids make your skin more sensitive to the sun. So if you are going out of your way to use glycolic acid to improve the quality of your skin, you also want to make sure you are protecting your skin from the sun, whether it's with sunprotective clothing or with sunscreen, so you don't undo all of the benefits. I also think it's wonderful if you can incorporate a body product that has retinol in it. I just talked about how amazing it is for your face. So, why wouldn't you want those same benefits for your body skin if you want something that's super affordable that you can get at pretty much any drugstore, Gold Bond makes a really nice retinol body lotion. The brand Dermatology also makes one that's a little bit more cosmetically elevated. So, it kind of depends on what your spend category is and what kind of texture you like with your body lotion. But, having a retinol body lotion at least a few times a week in your routine is definitely recommended by me. So, you've got really good sun protection, of course, that's going to be the foundation of a body care routine. Glycolic acid a couple times a week, a retinol body product a few times a week if possible, and then day-to-day, I do think it's nice to apply a general moisturizer that is going to support your skin as well. I'm a big fan of moisturizers that have ura. They help smooth and soften the skin. They help with hydration, and they help your other products penetrate the skin better. I am a huge fan, of course, of my prequel ura moisturizing milk. The brand Eucerin is also really popular for incorporating ura into their products. So think about ura if you haven't used a ura based product before. It doesn't sound sexy but it works. Now skincare alone is fantastic but of course I am a cosmetic dermatologist. I do inoff procedures and where I'm really seeing my patients take leaps in their skincare journey is when we are combining inoff treatments and often doing many treatments on one day. And my patients love this because they are busy. They want efficiency. And also, a lot of my patients come from fairly far away. They drive hours. And so, being able to do multiple things when they're in the office with appropriate planning. We can't just add on 10 things at the last minute can really make a difference in terms of seeing results quickly and making the best use of my patients time. And even though I've been a fan of combination treatments for a long time since I started my cosmetic practice, it was not something I would often bring up to my patients because I didn't want them to feel overwhelmed. But then over the years, I've had patients start to inquire, well, can I do my laser resurfacing and my micro needling on the same day? Can I do my filler and my Botox and my micro needling on the same day? And realizing that more people actually want to do those things. So, I talk about it a lot more as well. I also find that now that there are people on social media who talk about procedures, patients are a lot more familiar with what things look like, what the downtime might be, and so they're a lot less nervous about doing multiple things at once. Now, if I have a patient who is totally new to cosmetics, I often recommend doing treatments individually at first, so they can see how that feels in their body. They can see the individual side effects or downtime of that procedure on its own. But as they become more comfortable, as they understand that most of these don't have side effects, and recovery is really easy, they start to combine and make their trips to the dermatologist just more comprehensive. I will not bore you with all of the different treatment combinations we do in the office because it's very unique to each patient and what their goals are. But for example, for my patients with measma who are on a great medical regimen for their pigmentation but want to be boosting their skin, we will often combine clear and brilliant laser with micro needling, which is a very gentle resurfacing laser that kind of helps lift pigment. And then we do micro needling right afterward to go a bit deeper and stimulate collagen. So essentially they're having two procedures in one visit. they only have to numb one time and the recovery doesn't go way up in terms of intensity just because they've done two things. Instead of one to two days of downtime, they might have two to three days of downtime, which for most patients is worth it. I also will often combine IPL with Fraal or its little sister, Clear and Brilliant. IPL helps with things like pigmentation and redness. And then right afterward, we do a fractionated laser treatment with clear and brilliant for a gentle treatment, fraal for a more intense treatment to help with collagen stimulation and general resurfacing of the skin. And again, I have lots of patients who do IPL and then they do Fraal on a different day. But for people who want to be the most efficient with their time, they absolutely can be combined. And then for my patients who have a week of downtime who are ready to kind of do the most in a single treatment, I will often combine Pico laser with V-beam laser with frail where I'm using a pico laser to hit all of their darkest brown spots. I'm using a V-beam laser to hit all of their broken blood vessels and then I am frailing on top of everything to remove all of the pigmentation in the background. So, as you can see, it can kind of get complicated or complex, which is the whole reason we have consultations and we walk patients through this because we really want someone's treatment regimen to be personalized to them and what their goals are. And then, as I sort of alluded to before, many times patients can also get Botox or even get some of their fillers on the same day. This varies a little bit, but my general order of operations is I numb the patient, then we do fillers first. When they have no swelling, they're not read from their laser yet. I can see exactly where I need to precisely place that filler. Then they do any of their resurfacing treatments, so all of their lasers and micro needling. And then at the very end, if I get a sense that they're not going to have a lot of swelling, we do the Botox. And if you're listening to this and you are new to cosmetics, this might sound really overwhelming. And that again is the whole point of having a consultation. I also have tons of videos here on YouTube that break down these individual laser procedures, that break down Botox and fillers, so you can explore those as well. But I will say as someone who has been practicing cosmetic dermatology for a while is that ultimately whether you do it all on one day or on separate days, patients that do a little bit of everything, they're not leaning too much into laser or too much into Botox. They're getting small strategic amounts of Botox, wellplaced filler, a little bit of laser resurfacing. Those are the patients that look the most natural with their cosmetic procedures because you're addressing aging and the changes that they're seeing at all the different levels of the skin. And the goal is when you do these cosmetic treatments, you don't come out the other side looking unrecognizable. You look like the best version of yourself and you look natural. And that to me is my favorite part of being a cosmetic dermatologist is taking someone through this journey. Maybe they're a little nervous to start and they get to see their skin transform and what's really possible. And we see people get their confidence back, which to me is just the most satisfying thing. And sometimes for my patients that loss of confidence happens as they make a very specific transition in their life as they get a bit older and go through pmenopause and then menopause. And so one of the things I'm being really intentional about this year and have been working on for the last couple years is really supporting women through that pmenopausal transition. Menopause should not be this cliff that you metaphorically fall off of. And when it comes to dermatology, that is often the time when women come in and start talking about cosmetic procedures because they are noticing that all of the sudden their skin is sagging, their wrinkles are more prominent, the texture isn't what it was, or it's something that they can't quite pinpoint, but when they see themselves in the mirror or in a photo, they're not loving what they're seeing anymore. So, for a lot of my patients who are not only in menopause or post-menopause, but are approaching menopause in their late 30s and early 40s, I'm really talking with them about how to approach this transition in their life so that they don't feel like all of the sudden their skin makes a turn for the worse. One thing I'll focus with them a lot on is preserving the collagen that they have. So using topical retinoids, of course, diligent sun protection, but then using light treatments like gentle clear and brilliant laser or micro needling, things that don't have a ton of downtime and can generally fit fairly easily into your day-to-day life that are going to help you keep the collagen you have and make new collagen so that you're essentially banking up that collagen for when you approach menopause and that collagen suddenly wants to start dropping off. I also really encourage my patients to talk with their gynecologist about menopause hormone therapy or hormone replacement therapy. It is not something that I personally prescribe, but a lot of women don't know anything about it. They don't know that it's an option for them. And I feel like these are discussions that luckily are starting to happen more and more. But if I have a patient and they're like topical estrogen, never heard of it, like we are going to get into it. And by the way, if you have questions on topical estrogen, I have multiple videos here on YouTube to teach you how to use it and who it works best for. This is also a time where I start talking with my patients about buffing up their skincare routine. So, if they are doing the fundamentals with their antioxidant and their retinoid and their sun protection, we start thinking about adding things in like peptides, other molecules that can push their skin to be the best version of itself. You guys know I love my medicate liquid peptides, the advanced version in particular. I also love the one from May Love if you want a more affordable option. But adding a peptide serum to your routine is a really easy thing to do. And a lot of people will notice those changes and those improvements in their skin really quickly. And the final thing, and perhaps the most important, is talking with my patients about lifestyle interventions. I feel like so many times we're like, "Give me the laser, give me the cream, but what about sleeping an extra hour a night? What about implementing weight training? What about optimizing your diet?" These are huge fundamental things that support your health and well-being, which absolutely affects how your skin looks and feels, but people tend to put it on the back burner. So, it's really a time when you are approaching late 30s, early 40s, and beyond as a woman that you are and as a man, too. Like, I know you don't go through menopause, but still, it's really important that you are focusing on living your healthiest lifestyle. And it doesn't have to be a huge overhaul that all happens at once, but making those gradual changes will make the entire aging process more successful and it's going to complement anything that you are doing in your skincare routine or in an office like mine. So, these are the five recalls I'm really leaning into with my patients this year. They are things I have been implementing in the past, but we are going full out now. If you want me to deep dive specifically into any one of these things or talk about any of the products a little bit more, let me know in the comments. As always, thank you so much for being here. Take good care of your skin and I'll see you in the next video.

9DSG_pQdOKo.title: Which Brain Supplements Actually Work? (5 to Use, 3 to Avoid)
9DSG_pQdOKo.description: UNLOCK YOUR BRAIN'S FULL POTENTIAL! My free 2-minute quiz reveals your unique "Brain Operating System" and gives you ...
9DSG_pQdOKo.transcript: Have you ever walked into a room and completely forgotten why you were there? Or maybe you noticed that your memory isn't as sharp as it used to be. You're not alone. These moments happen to all of us, but they can feel more frequent when we're tired, stressed, or just stretched thin. So it's no surprise that many people turn to supplements hoping for that little extra mental edge. But how do you know what actually works? Today, I'm going to walk you through five supplements that have real science behind them for supporting memory, and also share three that may not be worth your time or money. I'm Dr. Tracey Marks, a psychiatrist, and I make mental health education videos to strengthen your mind, fortify your brain, and build resilience. And one way to do that is by understanding how nutrition and supplements impact cognitive health. Let's start with a quick reminder of why memory is important. Memory isn't just about remembering names or where you left your keys. It's essential for learning focus and emotional regulation, even decision making. When your memory functions well, you're more adaptable, more efficient, and better equipped to handle challenges. And while sleep, exercise, and nutrition form the foundation of brain health, the right supplements can provide extra support if you choose wisely. So let's look at the top five supplements for memory support. Number one, omega three fatty acids, specifically DHA and EPA, omega three fatty acids. And the two types DHA and EPA, play a major role in brain health. DHA in particular, makes up about 30% of the fat content of your brain. It's essential for maintaining flexibility and function of brain cell membranes, which affects how well your neurons communicate with each other. On the other hand, EPA helps regulate inflammation in the brain and something that, when left unchecked, can interfere with memory and focus over time. Multiple studies have shown that people with higher omega-three levels tend to perform better on memory tasks and experience slower cognitive decline with age. One meta-analysis, published in the "Journal of Alzheimer's Disease", found that omega-three supplements significantly improved memory in adults with mild memory complaints. The most effective daily dose appears to be between 1000 and 2000 mg of a combination of DHA and EPA, with a higher proportion of DHA for the memory support. If that's your goal, you can get omega-threes from fatty fish like salmon, sardines and mackerel, but it's hard to maintain a consistent intake through diet alone. And that's where supplementation can help. If you choose to supplement quality matters, look for products that clearly state the exact amount of DHA and EPA and not just, say, fish oil. Also, choose ones that are molecularly distilled to remove contaminants like mercury and chemical residues like PCBs. Also, opt for the triglyceride form over the ethyl ester form. Your body absorbs it more efficiently. Now, if you follow a plant- based diet, you're probably wondering: is there an option for me? And yes, there is algae oil supplements or vegan friendly and they're a source of DHA and EPA as well. Just know, though, you'll typically need a higher dose of algae oil to match the effectiveness of the fish oil. So it's even more important to check the label for how much DHA and EPA you're actually getting. Number two citicholine, also called CDP-choline. This compound helps your brain produce acetylcholine, a neurotransmitter essential for learning, attention, and short-term memory. But that's not all. It also helps support energy metabolism, which is how your brain generates and uses energy to stay focused, especially during long periods of thinking or problem solving. Citicholine is one of the few supplements that has been used as a prescription medication in Europe and Japan to treat cognitive impairments, including those caused by stroke or age related decline. But in the U.S., it's sold as an over-the-counter supplement. Research suggests that citicholine supplementation can improve memory support and overall cognitive performance, particularly in older adults with memory deficits or mild cognitive impairment. Some studies have also shown improvements in attention and recall when it's taken consistently. In terms of dosing, the effective range is typically between 250 and a thousand milligrams per day. Higher doses have been studied for specific medical conditions, but for general cognitive support, most people fall within that standard range. It's pretty well tolerated, and side effects are rare, but some people may experience mild stomach discomfort or a headache when they first start taking it. Number three is vitamin D. Most people think of vitamin D as important for bone health, but it also plays a vital role in brain function. Vitamin D receptors are located throughout the brain, including areas responsible for memory, focus, and mood regulation. Low levels of vitamin D have been linked to poor memory performance, slower processing speed, and even increased risk of cognitive decline in older adults. It also helps regulate key neurotransmitters like dopamine and serotonin, which affect both emotional stability and cognitive clarity. One noteworthy thing, though, about vitamin D is how common the deficiency is. Some studies estimate that up to 40% of Americans don't get enough vitamin D, and if you're among them, supplementation could make a real difference in your cognitive performance. Before jumping into a supplement, it's ideal to get your blood levels tested. If you are deficient, your health care provider may recommend higher doses like 5000 international units a day or more to bring your levels up initially. After that, most people maintain with a daily dose. Between 1000 and 2000 international units. When choosing a supplement, go with vitamin D3, not D2. D3 is generally better absorbed and more effective at raising and maintaining your vitamin D levels. And here's one more important tip your body needs magnesium to activate vitamin D without enough magnesium. You may not fully benefit from your supplement, so if you're supplementing with vitamin D, it's a good idea to make sure your magnesium intake is adequate, either from your diet or from a separate supplement number. For the B vitamins, specifically B6, B9, which is folate and B12. These three B vitamins work together to support brain function and protect against cognitive decline. One of their key roles is helping lower homocysteine levels, which is a compound that, when elevated, can damage blood vessels in the brain and is associated with memory problems and age related decline. Vitamin D is especially important. Even mild deficiencies can lead to symptoms like forgetfulness, brain fog, or even mood changes. And here's the thing: B vitamin deficiencies become more common as we age, particularly vitamin B12. And that's because B12 needs stomach acid for proper absorption, and stomach acid levels naturally decline in many older adults. Vegetarians and vegans are also at a higher risk of B12 deficiency, since it's found primarily in animal products. Because magnesium can have a calming effect, many people find it helpful to take the supplement in the evening. Now, let's shift gears for a moment and talk about a few supplements that are either overhyped, under-researched, or just not worth your investment, especially if your goal is to improve your memory. First up is Ginkgo biloba. This supplement has been around for decades and was once heavily marketed as a memory booster, especially for older adults. But more recent large scale studies have shown inconsistent results, and in healthy adults without any cognitive decline. The benefits are minimal at best. Additionally, ginkgo can interact with blood thinners and other medications, potentially increasing the risk of bleeding. So when you weigh the limited evidence against the possible side effects, it's clear there are better, safer options for supporting memory. Second is proprietary Brain Boost blends. You've probably seen these, the ones with a long scientific sounding ingredient lists and big claims about mental clarity or sharper focus. The problem is, many of these blends don't disclose the exact amount of each ingredient that makes it impossible to know whether you're actually getting a meaningful dose. A lot of these products use a tactic called fairy dusting, and that's where they include small traces of impressive ingredients like a Copa Mo'ne Aerie or Lion's Mane Mushroom just so that they can list them on the label. But the actual dose is far below what research shows is effective. So instead of these mystery blends, look for products that clearly list each ingredient with the exact dose so you can compare it to what's been used in clinical studies. That's how you can separate science from the hype. And then third is high caffeine nootropics stacks. Nootropics are substances that aim to enhance brain function like focus, memory, or mental clarity. But many of these products rely heavily on caffeine to deliver that quick, noticeable boost. These products are often sold as stacks, which just means it's a combination of ingredients designed to work together. In this case, it's usually caffeine, plus other stimulants or exotic compounds. While caffeine contemporarily improve alertness, it's not a true memory enhancer, and when it starts to interfere with your sleep, which is often the case, it can actually impair your memory consolidation, which is the process your brain uses to store new information. That big energy spike might feel like it's improved brain performance. But over time, it comes with diminishing returns like jitteriness or racing heart or poor sleep are common side effects, and ironically, those things worsen memory and focus over time. So if you're looking for a sustainable support for your brain, it's best to skip the stimulant heavy stacks and focus on nutrients that actually support the structure and long term function of your brain. So how should you actually approach supplements for memory support? Let's talk about how to take all of this information and apply it in a way that makes sense and gets results. Start with the basics before adding any supplements. Make sure your foundation is solid. That means having a nutrient rich diet, physical activity that's regular and quality sleep, and some kind of stress management practice as well. Supplements work best when they're supporting a healthy lifestyle, not to compensate for an unhealthy one. Then consider testing for deficiencies. And this is especially important for vitamin D and vitamin B12. If you're low in either one, correcting that deficiency can make a big difference in how your brain functions. Your health care provider can order those tests and help interpret the results for you, so you can know where you stand. You want to add supplements one at a time. If you start multiple things at once, it's hard to know what's actually helping. So give each supplement a few weeks, ideally a full month, before adding something new. That way, you can clearly see what's working and what's not. Another thing you should do is track your results. Rather than going off a vague sense of I think I feel better. You also want to choose high quality products. Look for supplements that are third party tested by organizations like USP, NSF, or Consumer Lab. o0vdOA2QJ_4.transcript: Alpha Brain claims to enhance memory and focus but does it really deliver on any of these claims a big problem with supplements that claim to boost your brain is the lack of human research supporting their efficacy while some ingredients may have undergone human studies much of this research focuses on those with cognitive impairments like Alzheimer's disease or dementia rather than on healthy individuals it's important to recognize this distinction because if you're looking to boost your cognitive abilities and you don't have any diagnosed cognitive problems many of these supplements are unlikely to help you keep this in mind as we analyze the ingredients in on it Alpha Brain vitamin B6 plays a role in brain and nerve Health alongside other B vitamins like B12 and folic acid it's involved the neurotransmitter synthesis and the formation of myin which is essential for proper nerve function however Research indicates that supplementing with vitamin B6 does not benefit mood or cognitive function in fact the same goes for B12 and folic acid supplementation with any of these B vitamins don't seem to enhance cognitive abilities either furthermore taking B vitamin supplements doesn't seem to prevent cognitive decline nor slow down the cognitive decline of older individuals so despite the essential functions that B vitamins provide for our cognitive well-being studies consistently show that supplementing more of them does not boost cognitive function or slow down cognitive decline why is this the case well our bodies only need a certain amount of B vitamins and exceeding that amount doesn't offer any extra cognitive health benefits and B vitamin deficiencies are not very common especially in places like North America L tyrosine an amino acid shows some potential for enhancing cognitive function especially in individuals dealing with stress or anxiety Research indicates that taking Tire scene can improve cognitive performance under noisy conditions as well as boost cognition during periods of sleep deprivation however to experience these benefits very high doses are needed typically around 150 mg per kilog of body weight for instance a 70 kg or 155 lb adult would require over 10 G of tyrosine to see these effects that's about 2 teaspoons of tyrosine this high dosage requirement is why most combination supplements containing L tyrosine for cognitive enhancement like Alpha Brain fall far short in their dosing rarely providing enough tyrosine to make a noticeable difference alanine and amino acid naturally occurring in green tea has some potential to enhance certain aspects of cognitive function although the evidence is weak Studies have shown that supplementing with alanine appear to decrease error rates in cognitive tests when compared to a placebo however while eleanine or caffeine alone boosted cognitive performance combining them did not yield additional benefits Beyond just taking the caffeine by itself additionally it's important to note that not all Studies have found cognitive benefits associated with taking Aline over a placebo limited research exists on a particular wild Green Oats extract indicating a potential Improvement in overall cognitive speed but not accuracy when compared to a placebo however this study used a specific proprietary oat extract known as nurina administered at a large dose of 800 mg neither of which are used in alphab brain phospha Serene plays a crucial role as a building block for nerve cells and holds some promise in addressing cognitive problems research involving individuals with cognitive impairment show that taking 100 Mig of phosphole serene three times daily for up to 6 months May enhance various cognitive functions in the case of Alzheimer's disease phosph serans seem to offer temporary improvements in certain aspects of brain function however these benefits appear to diminish after 16 weeks as the disease progressed so while there is evidence that phosphole seran can at least temporarily improve cognition in those with Alzheimer's or cognitive impairment there's no indication that it provides similar benefits to healthy individuals ual there's no human evidence suggesting that cat's claw or any of its extracts can enhance or support cognitive function typically it's used for arthritis and even then there's limited evidence to support it's used for that I'm really not sure why it was included in Alpha Brain at all Alpha GPC a form of choline is a nutrient naturally occurring in foods like eggs while there's evidence suggesting that Alpha GPC could enhance cognitive function in Alzheimer's patients it requireed doses of 1,200 mg daily for up to 6 months well beyond what Alpha Brain provides moreover these effects were specific to individuals with Alzheimer's not healthy patients the idea that Alpha GPC might improve cognition in healthy adults stems from the belief that it acts as a precursor to acetyl choline a neurotransmitter some believe that consistent use of alpha GPC could raise acetylcholine levels potentially benefiting cognitive functions however this Theory lacks support from any human trials and there's no scientific evidence to show that this benefit exists for individuals without Alzheimer's in fact prolonged use of alpha GBC supplements have been linked to a higher risk of stroke therefore unless you have a specific condition like Alzheimer's that warrants its use I'd probably try to avoid using Alpha GBC but Copa is a herb that shows some po potential for enhancing certain cognitive aspects in healthy adults although the research findings are inconsistent however the doses examined in studies involving healthy adults typically range in the hundreds of milligrams per day which is much more than what Alpha Brain offers Hine a extracted from plants like Chinese Club Moss is known as an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor in simpler terms it blocks an enzyme that breaks down acetylcholine resulting in higher levels of of this neurotransmitter in the brain this increase in a seal choline May improve communication between neurons potentially enhancing cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease hubin a has shown promise in enhancing memory cognitive function and behavior it's commonly used in China for treating this condition however for healthy adults hin a is unlikely to have a significant impact on cognitive function furthermore the use of acetylon esteras Inhibitors like hubis a does not appear to provide notable benefits for individuals without Alzheimer's disease Lucine is an amino acid that doesn't really impact cognitive function but it's one of the few branched chain amino acids or BCAAs BCAAs are thought to promote muscle protein synthesis aiding in muscle repair and growth they also provide energy for active muscles helping to Stave off fatigue during intense physical activity however getting a useful dose requires a lot typically in gram amounts the tiny quantity present in Alpha Brain is unlikely to have any noticeable effect terot still Bean is an antioxidant compound primarily found in blueberries while whole blueberries have been studied for potential cognitive benefits with some weak evidence suggesting they might help there's no human research indicating that the specific compound teros still Bean has any effect on cognitive function so let's break down what we've discussed first we'll eliminate ingredients without any human evidence supporting cognitive Improvement or real benefits in humans next let's consider ingredients with benefits for cognitive performance but primarily in individuals with cognitive issues like dementia these likely won't be very helpful for those who are healthy now let's identify ingredients that have shown potential cognitive benefits for healthy individuals finally let's eliminate all ingredients using doses too low to provide might any benefit this leaves us with two potentially useful ingredients Eline and Hine among these only Aline a compound found in green tea offers some benefits for healthy adults the remaining ingredients either don't improve
